entry.content:
In this article, we will introduce some basic results of gradient flows in the Euclidean space.

Given a function $F:\mathbb{R}^n o \mathbb{R}$, smooth enough and a point $x_0 \in \mathbb{R}^n$. A gradient flow is just defined as a curve $X(t)$, with starting point at $t=0$ given by $x_0$, which moves by choosing at each instant of time the direction which makes the function $F$ decrease as much as possible. More precisely, we consider the solution of the Cauchy problem
$$
egin{cases}
x’(t) = - abla F(x(t)) & ext{for } t>0, \\
x(0)=x_0
\end{cases}
ag{1}
$$

This is a standard Cauchy problem which has a unique solution if $ abla F$ is Lipschitz continuous.

If $F$ is convex, we can replace the gradient with subdifferential, looking for absolutely continuous curve $x:[0,T] o \mathbb{R}^n$
$$
egin{cases}
x’(t)\in -\partial F(x(t)) & ext{for a.e. } t>0, \\
x(0)=x_0
\end{cases}
ag{2}
$$
where
$$
\partial F(x):=\left\{ p\in \mathbb{R}^n \mid F(y)\ge F(x)+p\cdot (y-x)\ ext{for all } y\in \mathbb{R}^n ight\}.
$$

Properties of subdifferential

  1. $F$ is differentiable at $x$ iff $\partial F(x)=\{ abla F(x)\}$.
  2. $\partial F(x)$ is a convex set.
  3. $\partial F(x)$ is non-empty for $x\in \operatorname{dom} F$.
  4. For every $x_1,x_2$ and $p_1\in \partial F(x_1)$, $p_2\in \partial F(x_2)$,
    $$
    (x_1-x_2)\cdot (p_1-p_2)\ge 0.
    $$

We denote by $\partial^\circ F(x)$ its element of minimal norm.

Proposition 1. Suppose that $F$ is convex and let $x_1,x_2$ be two solutions of (2). Then we have
$$
|x_1(t)-x_2(t)|\le |x_1(0)-x_2(0)|
$$
for every $t\ge 0$. In particular, this gives uniqueness of solution of the Cauchy problem.

Proof. Let us consider
$$
g(t)= rac12 |x_1(t)-x_2(t)|^2.
$$
Then we have
$$
g’(t)=(x_1(t)-x_2(t))\cdot (x_1’(t)-x_2’(t)).
$$
Since $x_1’(t)\in -\partial F(x_1(t))$, $x_2’(t)\in -\partial F(x_2(t))$, we have
$$
g’(t)\le 0.
$$
Therefore
$$
g(t)\le g(0).\quad \square
$$

Remark. We recall that $F$ is semi-convex means that there exists $\lambda\in \mathbb{R}$ such that
$$
x\mapsto F(x)- rac{\lambda}{2}|x|^2
$$
is convex.

For $\lambda$-convex function $F$, we can define the subdifferential by
$$
\partial F(x)=\left\{ p\in \mathbb{R}^n \ \middle| \ F(y)\ge F(x)+p\cdot (y-x)+ rac{\lambda}{2}|y-x|^2 \ ext{for all } y\in \mathbb{R}^n ight\}.
$$

Again, we define $\partial^\circ F$ the element of minimal norm of $\partial F$.

If $F$ is $\lambda$-convex, then for $x_1,x_2,p_1,p_2$ with $p_i\in \partial F(x_i)$, $i=1,2$, one has
$$
(x_1-x_2)\cdot (p_1-p_2)\ge \lambda |x_1-x_2|^2.
$$

This implies
$$
g’(t)\le -2\lambda g(t).
$$
By Gronwall’s inequality
$$
g(t)\le g(0)e^{-2\lambda t}.
$$

If $\lambda>0$, then $F$ is strictly convex and admits a unique minimizer $ ar{x}$.

Take $x_2(t)\equiv ar{x}$, which is also a solution since $0\in \partial F( ar{x})$. Then we get
$$
|x_1(t)- ar{x}|\le e^{-\lambda t}|x_1(0)- ar{x}|.
$$

Proposition 2. Suppose that $F$ is $\lambda$-convex and let $x$ be a solution of (2). Then, for all times $t_0$ such that both $t\mapsto x(t)$ and $t\mapsto F(x(t))$ are differentiable at $t=t_0$, the subdifferential $\partial F(x(t_0))$ is contained in a hyperplane orthogonal to $x’(t_0)$. In particular, we have
$$
x’(t)=-\partial^\circ F(x(t)) \qquad ext{for a.e. } t.
$$

Proof. Let $t_0$ be as in the statement and $p\in \partial F(x(t_0))$. By the definition of subdifferential
$$
F(x(t))\ge F(x(t_0))+p\cdot (x(t)-x(t_0))+ rac{\lambda}{2}|x(t)-x(t_0)|^2
\qquad ext{for all } t,
$$
and it is equality when $t=t_0$. Hence
$$
t\mapsto F(x(t))-F(x(t_0))-p\cdot (x(t)-x(t_0))- rac{\lambda}{2}|x(t)-x(t_0)|^2
$$
is minimal for $t=t_0$, and then
$$
rac{d}{dt}F(x(t))\Big|_{t=t_0}=p\cdot x’(t_0).
$$
Hence
$$
\partial F(x(t_0))\subseteq \left\{ p\in \mathbb{R}^n \mid p\cdot x’(t_0)=\mathrm{const} ight\},
$$
which is a hyperplane orthogonal to $x’(t_0)$.

Since for a.e. $t_0$,
$$
-x’(t_0)\in \partial F(x(t_0)),
$$
we have
$$
\mathrm{const}=-x’(t_0)\cdot x’(t_0)=-|x’(t_0)|^2.
$$
The hyperplane is
$$
\left\{ p\in \mathbb{R}^n \mid p\cdot x’(t_0)=-|x’(t_0)|^2 ight\}.
$$
Hence the orthogonal projection of $0$ onto the hyperplane which contains $\partial F(x(t_0))$ is
$$
rac{x’(t_0)}{|x’(t_0)|^2}\cdot ig(-|x’(t_0)|^2 ig)=-x’(t_0).
$$
This provides
$$
x’(t_0)=-\partial^\circ F(x(t_0))
$$
for a.e. $t_0$. $\square$

Discretization in time

Fix a small step time parameter $ au>0$, define

$$
x_{k+1}^ au \in \operatorname*{argmin} _x \left\{ F(x)+ rac{|x-x_k^ au|^2}{2 au} ight\}.
ag{3}
$$

and we say $(x_k^ au) _k$ the Minimizing Movement Scheme.

If $F$ is smooth, then

$$
x _{k+1}^ au \in \operatorname*{argmin} _x \left\{ F(x)+ rac{| x-x_k^ au |^2}{2 au} ight\}
\implies
abla F(x _{k+1}^ au)+ rac{x _{k+1}^ au-x _k^ au}{ au}=0.
$$

Hence,
$$
rac{x_{k+1}^ au-x_k^ au}{ au}=- abla F(x_{k+1}^ au),
$$
which is exactly the discrete-time implicit Euler scheme.

Given ODE
$$
egin{cases}
x’(t)=V(x(t)),\\
x(0)=x_0,
\end{cases}
$$

  • Explicit scheme
    $$
    x_{k+1}^ au=x_k^ au+ au V(x_k^ au), \qquad x_0=x_0.
    $$

  • Implicit scheme
    $$
    x_{k+1}^ au=x_k^ au+ au V(x_{k+1}^ au), \qquad x_0=x_0.
    $$

Now, we want to show that for $ au o 0$, the sequence $(x_k^ au)$ we found, suitably interpolated, converges to the solution of (2).

First, define
$$
v_{k+1}^ au:= rac{x_{k+1}^ au-x_k^ au}{ au}.
$$
Then we set
$$
x^ au(t):=x_{k+1}^ au, \qquad ext{for } t\in (k au,(k+1) au],
$$
$$
ilde x^ au(t):=x_k^ au+(t-k au)v_{k+1}^ au, \qquad ext{for } t\in (k au,(k+1) au],
$$
$$
v^ au(t):=v_{k+1}^ au, \qquad ext{for } t\in (k au,(k+1) au].
$$

It is easy to see that $ ilde x^ au$ is continuous and pairwise affine, satisfying
$$
( ilde x^ au)’=v^ au.
$$

What’s more, $x^ au$ is not continuous, but by definition
$$
v^ au(t)\in -\partial F(x^ au(t)).
$$

By the definition of $x_{k+1}^ au$, we have
$$
F(x_{k+1}^ au)+ rac{|x_{k+1}^ au-x_k^ au|^2}{2 au}\le F(x_k^ au).
ag{4}
$$

If $F(x_0)<+\infty$ and $\inf F>-\infty$, summing over $k$, we get
$$
\sum_{k=0}^{\ell} rac{|x_{k+1}^ au-x_k^ au|^2}{2 au}
\le
ig(F(x_0^ au)-F(x_{\ell+1}^ au) ig)\le C.
ag{5}
$$

Note that
$$
rac{|x_{k+1}^ au-x_k^ au|^2}{2 au}
=
rac{ au}{2}\left| rac{x_{k+1}^ au-x_k^ au}{ au} ight|^2
=
rac{ au}{2}|v_{k+1}^ au|^2
=
rac12\int_{k au}^{(k+1) au}|( ilde x^ au)’(t)|^2,dt.
$$

This means that we have
$$
\int_0^T rac12 |( ilde x^ au)’(t)|^2,dt\le C.
ag{6}
$$

Hence $ ilde x^ au$ is bounded in $H^1$ and $v^ au$ in $L^2$. By Hölder inequality, for $t\ge s$,
$$
| ilde x^ au(t)- ilde x^ au(s)|
=
\left|\int_s^t ( ilde x^ au)’(u),du ight|
\le
\int_s^t |( ilde x^ au)’(u)|,du
\le
\left(\int_s^t |( ilde x^ au)’(u)|^2,du ight)^{1/2}|t-s|^{1/2}
\le C|t-s|^{1/2}.
ag{7}
$$

Therefore $ ilde x^ au$ is equicontinuous for all $ au>0$. Moreover,
$$
| ilde x^ au(t)-x_0|
=
| ilde x^ au(t)- ilde x^ au(0)|
\le Ct^{1/2}\le CT^{1/2}.
$$
Hence $ ilde x^ au$ is also equibounded. Furthermore, since $x^ au(t)= ilde x^ au(s)$ for a certain $s=k au$ with $|s-t|\le au$, we have
$$
|x^ au(t)- ilde x^ au(t)|\le C au^{1/2}.
ag{8}
$$

Proposition 3. Let $ ilde x^ au$, $x^ au$ and $v^ au$ be constructed as above using the minimizing movement scheme. Suppose $F(x_0)<+\infty$ and $\inf F>-\infty$, and lower-semicontinuous. Then, up to a subsequence $ au_j o 0$ (still denoted by $ au$), both $ ilde x^ au$ and $x^ au$ converge uniformly to a same curve $x\in H^1$, and $v^ au$ weakly converges in $L^2$ to a vector function $v$ such that $x’=v$ and

  1. if $F$ is $\lambda$-convex, we have
    $$
    v(t)\in -\partial F(x(t)) \qquad ext{for a.e. } t,
    $$
    i.e. $x$ is a solution of (2);

  2. if $F$ is $C^1$, we have
    $$
    v(t)=- abla F(x(t)) \qquad ext{for all } t,
    $$
    i.e. $x$ is a solution of (1).

Proof. By the above analysis, $ ilde x^ au$ is continuous, equicontinuous and equibounded. By applying Arzelà–Ascoli theorem, we get a uniformly converging subsequence (still denoted by $ au$)
$$
ilde x^{ au} o x
\qquad ext{as } au o 0
$$

By (8), $x^ au$ also uniformly converges to the same limit $x$. Moreover by (6), we know that $v^ au$ is equibounded in $L^2$ which is a reflexive Banach space. Thus, up to an extra subsequence extraction (still denoted by $ au$),
$$
v^ au ightharpoonup v \qquad ext{in } L^2 \quad ext{as } au o 0.
$$

Therefore, as a consequence of distributional convergence,
$$
x’=v.
$$

To prove (1), fix a point $y\in \mathbb{R}^n$. By $v^ au(t)\in -\partial F(x^ au(t))$, we have
$$
F(y)\ge F(x^ au(t)) - v^ au(t)\cdot (y-x^ au(t)) + rac{\lambda}{2}|y-x^ au(t)|^2.
$$

For all positive measurable function $a:[0,T] o \mathbb{R}_+$,
$$
\int_0^T a(t)\left( F(y)-F(x^ au(t))+v^ au(t)\cdot (y-x^ au(t))- rac{\lambda}{2}|y-x^ au(t)|^2 ight),dt \ge 0.
$$

Since $x^ au o x$ uniformly and hence $L^2$ strong, $v^ au ightharpoonup v$, and $F$ is lower-semicontinuous, by letting $ au o 0$, we get
$$
\int_0^T a(t)\left( F(y)-F(x(t))+v(t)\cdot (y-x(t))- rac{\lambda}{2}|y-x(t)|^2 ight),dt \ge 0.
$$

From the arbitrariness of $a$, we get
$$
F(y)\ge F(x(t)) - v(t)\cdot (y-x(t)) + rac{\lambda}{2}|y-x(t)|^2
\qquad ext{for a.e. } t.
$$

i.e. there exists a negligible set $N_y$ such that
$$
G_y(t):=F(y)-F(x(t))+v(t)\cdot (y-x(t))- rac{\lambda}{2}|y-x(t)|^2 \ge 0
\qquad ext{for } t otin N_y.
$$

Let $D$ be a countable dense set in the interior of $\operatorname{dom}F$, where $F$ is continuous, and
$$
N= igcup_{y\in D}N_y
$$
which is also negligible. For all $t otin N$, and all $y\in \operatorname{dom}F$, there exists $y_m\in D$ such that $y_m o y$ as $m o\infty$ and then
$$
G_y(t)=\lim_{m o\infty} G_{y_m}(t)\ge 0
\qquad ext{for all } t otin N.
$$
Hence,
$$
v(t)\in -\partial F(x(t)).
$$

To prove (2), we have
$$
- abla F(x^ au(t))=v^ au(t)=( ilde x^ au)’(t).
$$
Since $x^ au o x$ uniformly and $F\in C^1$, we get, $v^ au ightharpoonup v$, we get
$$
v(t)=- abla F(x(t)) \qquad ext{a.e. } t.
$$
Since $t\mapsto - abla F(x(t))$ is uniformly continuous, the above equality holds for all $t$. $\square$

Remark. If the sequence $x_k^ au$ is defined by

$$
x_{k+1}^ au \in \operatorname*{argmin} _x \left\{ 2F\left( rac{x+x _k^ au}{2} ight)+ rac{|x-x _k^ au |^2}{2 au} ight\},
$$

then we have

$$
rac{x_{k+1}^ au-x_k^ au}{ au}
=
- abla F\left( rac{x_{k+1}^ au+x_k^ au}{2} ight),
$$

and the convergence is of order $ au^2$.

Reference

Santambrogio, F. {Euclidean, metric, and Wasserstein} gradient flows: an overview. Bull. Math. Sci. 7, 87–154 (2017).

The cover image in this article was taken in Hobbiton, New Zealand.
entry.summary: In this article, we will introduce some basic results of gradient flows in the Euclidean space.
entry.title: Gradient Flows in the Euclidean Space

entry.content:
In this article, we will introduce monotone transport maps and plans in 1D.

Definition 2.1 Given a nondecreasing and right continuous function $F:\mathbb{R} o[0,1]$, its pseudo-inverse is the function $F^{[-1]}:[0,1] o\overline{\mathbb{R}}$ given by
$$
F^{[-1]}(x)=\inf\{t\in\mathbb{R}\mid F(t)\ge x\}.
$$
Thanks to right continuity of $F$, the infimum is minimum as soon as the set is nonempty.

Lemma 2.1 We have

  1. $F^{[-1]}(x)\le a \iff F(a)\ge x$,
  2. $F^{[-1]}(x)>a \iff F(a)<x$.

Proof : We only prove (1). If $F^{[-1]}(x)\le a$, then the set
$$
\{t\in\mathbb{R}\mid F(t)\ge x\} eq\varnothing
$$
and then the infimum is minimum. Suppose $F(a)<x$, then for each $b\le a$, $F(b)\le F(a)<x$, hence
$$
b otin\{t\in\mathbb{R}\mid F(t)\ge x\}\Rightarrow (-\infty,a]\subset\{t\in\mathbb{R}\mid F(t)<x\}.
$$
Hence by definition, $F^{[-1]}(x)>a$, which gives a contradiction. Conversely, if $F(a)\ge x$, it is obvious that $F^{[-1]}(x)\le a$. $\square$

Proposition 2.1 If $\mu\in\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$ and $F_\mu^{[-1]}$ is the pseudo-inverse of its cumulative distribution function $F_\mu$, then

$$
(F_\mu^{[-1]}) _{\sharp} ig(\mathcal{L}^1\llcorner[0,1] ig) =\mu.
$$

Moreover, given $\mu, u\in\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$, if we set
$$
\eta:= ig(F_\mu^{[-1]},F_ u^{[-1]} ig)_{\sharp} ig(\mathcal{L}^1\llcorner[0,1] ig),
$$
then $\eta\in\Pi(\mu, u)$ and
$$
\eta ig((-\infty,a] imes(-\infty,b] ig)=F_\mu(a)\wedge F_ u(b).
$$

Proof :
$$
\mathcal{L}^1 ig(\{x\in[0,1]\ ;\ F_\mu^{[-1]}(x)\le a\} ig)
=\mathcal{L}^1 ig(\{x\in[0,1]\ ;\ x\le F_\mu(a)\} ig)=F_\mu(a).
$$
Hence
$$
\{(-\infty,a)\ ;\ a\in\mathbb{R}\}\subset \mathcal{G}:=\{A\in\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})\ ;\ (\mathcal{L}^1\llcorner[0,1])((F_\mu^{[-1]})^{-1}(A))=\mu(A)\}.
$$
By monotone class theorem, we get $\mathcal{G}=\mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R})$, which gives the desired result. Moreover $\eta\in\Pi(\mu, u)$ is just a consequence of the above statement.

Further,
$$
\eta((-\infty,a] imes(-\infty,b])
=\mathcal{L}^1 ig(\{x\in[0,1]\ ;\ F_\mu^{[-1]}(x)\le a,\ F_ u^{[-1]}(x)\le b\} ig)
$$
and by Lemma 2.1 this equals
$$
\mathcal{L}^1 ig(\{x\in[0,1]\ ;\ x\le F_\mu(a),\ x\le F_ u(b)\} ig)
=F_\mu(a)\wedge F_ u(b),
$$
which is the desired equality. $\square$

Definition 2.2 We will call the transport plan

$$
\eta:= ig(F_\mu^{[-1]},F_ u^{[-1]} ig) _{\sharp} ig(\mathcal{L}^1\llcorner[0,1] ig)
$$

the co-monotone transport plan between $\mu$ and $ u$ and denote it by
$$
\gamma_{\mathrm{mon}}:=\eta.
$$

Lemma 2.2 If $\mu\in\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$ is atomless, then $(F_\mu)_{\sharp}\mu=\mathcal{L}^1\llcorner[0,1]$. As a consequence, for every $\ell\in[0,1]$, the set $\{x:F_\mu(x)=\ell\}$ is $\mu$-negligible.

Proof : First, $F_\mu$ is continuous since $\mu$ is atomless. Hence for $a\in(0,1)$, the set
$$
\{x\ ;\ F_\mu(x)\le a\}=(-\infty,x_a],
$$
where $F_\mu(x_a)=a$. Hence
$$
\mu ig(F_\mu^{-1}((-\infty,a]) ig)=\mu((-\infty,x_a])=F_\mu(x_a)=a.
$$
Therefore

$$
(F_\mu) _{\sharp}\mu=\mathcal{L}^1\llcorner[0,1].
$$

As a consequence, for $\ell\in[0,1]$, the set
$$
\{x\ ;\ F_\mu(x)=\ell\}
$$
is $\mu$-negligible. Otherwise, if
$$
\mu(\{x\ ;\ F_\mu(x)=\ell\})>0,
$$
then
$$
0=\mathcal{L}^1(\{\ell\})=(F_\mu)_{\sharp}\mu(\{\ell\})>0,
$$
which is a contradiction. $\square$

Theorem 2.1 Given $\mu, u\in\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$, suppose that $\mu$ is atomless. Then there exists a unique nondecreasing map $T_{\mathrm{mon}}:\mathbb{R} o\mathbb{R}$ such that
$$
(T_{\mathrm{mon}})_{\sharp}\mu= u.
$$

Proof : Define
$$
T_{\mathrm{mon}}(x):=F_ u^{[-1]}(F_\mu(x)).
$$
By Lemma 2.2, it is easy to see that $T_{\mathrm{mon}}$ is well-defined. The fact that $T_{\mathrm{mon}}$ is monotone nondecreasing is obvious. Now, we only need to show $(T_{\mathrm{mon}})_{\sharp}\mu= u$.

In fact,

$$
(T_{\mathrm{mon}}) _{\sharp}\mu
=(F_ u^{[-1]}\circ F_\mu) _{\sharp}\mu
=(F_ u^{[-1]}) _{\sharp} ig((F_\mu) _{\sharp}\mu ig)
=(F_ u^{[-1]}) _{\sharp} ig(\mathcal{L}^1\llcorner[0,1] ig)= u.
$$

Finally, we need to show the uniqueness. From monotonicity,
$$
T^{-1}((-\infty,T(x)])\supset (-\infty,x].
$$
We deduce
$$
F_\mu(x)=\mu((-\infty,x])\le \mu ig(T^{-1}((-\infty,T(x)]) ig)
= u((-\infty,T(x)])=F_ u(T(x)),
$$
which means
$$
T(x)\ge F_ u^{[-1]}(F_\mu(x)).
$$
Suppose $T(x)>F_ u^{[-1]}(F_\mu(x))$. This means there exists $\varepsilon_0>0$ such that
$$
F_ u(T(x)-\varepsilon)\ge F_\mu(x)\qquad ext{for all }\varepsilon\in(0,\varepsilon_0).
$$
On the other hand, from
$$
T^{-1}((-\infty,T(x)-\varepsilon])\subset (-\infty,x],
$$
we get
$$
F_ u(T(x)-\varepsilon)= u((-\infty,T(x)-\varepsilon])
=\mu ig(T^{-1}((-\infty,T(x)-\varepsilon]) ig)\le \mu((-\infty,x])=F_\mu(x).
$$
Therefore
$$
F_ u(T(x)-\varepsilon)=F_\mu(x)\qquad ext{for every }\varepsilon\in(0,\varepsilon_0).
$$
Thus $F_ u(T(x)-\varepsilon)$ is the value that $F_ u$ takes on an interval where it is constant. These intervals are countable, hence the values of $F_ u$ on those intervals are also countable. We can call $\ell_i$ these values. As a consequence, the points $x$ where
$$
T(x)>F_ u^{[-1]}(F_\mu(x))
$$
are contained in
$$
igcup_i \{x\ ;\ F_\mu(x)=\ell_i\},
$$
which is $\mu$-negligible. Thus, $T(x)=F_ u^{[-1]}(F_\mu(x))$ $\mu$-a.e. $\square$

Lemma 2.3 Let $\gamma\in\Pi(\mu, u)$ be a transport plan between two measures $\mu, u\in\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$. Suppose that it satisfies the property:
$$
(x,y),(x’,y’)\in\operatorname{Supp}(\gamma),\ x<x’ \Rightarrow y\le y’. ag{1}
$$
Then we have $\gamma=\gamma_{\mathrm{mon}}$. In particular, there is a unique $\gamma$ satisfying (1). Moreover, if $\mu$ is atomless, then $\gamma=T_{\mathrm{mon}}$.

Proof : First, we need to prove
$$
\gamma((-\infty,a] imes(-\infty,b])=F_\mu(a)\wedge F_ u(b).
$$
Consider
$$
A:=(-\infty,a] imes(b,+\infty),\qquad B:=(a,+\infty) imes(-\infty,b].
$$
Claim: It is not possible to have both $\gamma(A)>0$ and $\gamma(B)>0$.

Indeed, if not, $\gamma(A)>0$ and $\gamma(B)>0$, then for some $(x,y)\in A$ and $(x’,y’)\in B$ we have
$$
x<x’,\qquad y>y’,
$$
with $(x,y),(x’,y’)\in\operatorname{Supp}(\gamma)$, which contradicts (1). Therefore,
$$
\gamma((-\infty,a] imes(-\infty,b])
=\gamma ig(((-\infty,a] imes\mathbb{R})\setminus A ig)
\wedge
\gamma ig((\mathbb{R} imes(-\infty,b])\setminus B ig)
$$
and hence
$$
\gamma((-\infty,a] imes(-\infty,b])=F_\mu(a)\wedge F_ u(b).
$$
Hence $\gamma=\gamma_{\mathrm{mon}}$.

Now, assume $\gamma$ is atomless, then we can define $I_x$ as the minimal interval such that
$$
\operatorname{Supp}(\gamma)\cap(\{x\} imes\mathbb{R})\subset \{x\} imes I_x.
$$
The assumption (1) shows that the interior of all these intervals are disjoint. There can be at most countable points such that $I_x$ is not singleton. Since $\mu$ is atomless, we can define a map $T$ such that $\gamma$ is concentrated on graph of $T$, $\mu$-a.e. By Theorem 2.1, $T=T_{\mathrm{mon}}$. $\square$

Reference

Santambrogio, Filippo. Optimal transport for applied mathematicians. Birkäuser, NY 55.58-63 (2015): 94.

The cover image of this article was taken in Sydney, Australia.
entry.summary: In this article, we will introduce monotone transport maps and plans in 1D.
entry.title: Monotone Transport Maps and Plans in 1D

entry.content:
In this article we will introduce the definition of Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS).

Let $E$ be a non empty set. Let $\mathcal H$ be a vector space of functions defined on $E$ and taking their values in the set $\mathbb C$ of complex numbers. $\mathcal H$ is endowed with the structure of Hilbert space defined by an inner product $\langle \cdot,\cdot\rangle_{\mathcal H}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal H\times \mathcal H &\longrightarrow \mathbb C\\\
(\varphi,\psi)&\longmapsto \langle \varphi,\psi\rangle_{\mathcal H}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $\Vert\cdot\Vert_{\mathcal H}$ denote the associated norm,

$$
\forall \varphi\in\mathcal H,\qquad \Vert\varphi\Vert_{\mathcal H}=\langle \varphi,\varphi\rangle_{\mathcal H}^{1/2}.
$$

For any $t\in E$, we will denote by $e_t$ the evaluation functional at the point $t$, i.e. the mapping

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_t:\mathcal H&\longrightarrow \mathbb C\\\
g&\longmapsto e_t(g):=g(t).
\end{aligned}
$$

Definition 1. (Reproducing kernel) A function

$$
\begin{aligned}
K:E\times E&\longrightarrow \mathbb C\\\
(s,t)&\longmapsto K(s,t)
\end{aligned}
$$

is a reproducing kernel of the Hilbert space $\mathcal H$ if and only if

(a) $\forall t\in E,\quad K(\cdot,t)\in\mathcal H$,

(b) $\forall t\in E,\ \forall \varphi\in\mathcal H$,

$$
\langle \varphi,K(\cdot,t)\rangle_{\mathcal H}=\varphi(t).
$$

Note. The property (b) is called the “reproducing property”: the value of $\varphi$ at point $t$ is reproduced by the inner product of $\varphi$ with $K(\cdot,t)$.

Remark: It is clear that $\forall (s,t)\in E\times E$,

$$
K(s,t)=\langle K(\cdot,t),K(\cdot,s)\rangle.
$$

A Hilbert space of complex-valued functions which possesses a reproducing kernel is called a “reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS)”, or a “proper Hilbert space”.

Example 1: Let $e_1,\ldots,e_n$ be an orthonormal basis in $\mathcal H$ and define

$$
K(x,y)=\sum_{i=1}^n e_i(x)\overline{e_i(y)}.
$$

Then for any $y\in E$,

$$
K(\cdot,y)=\sum_{i=1}^n \overline{e_i(y)},e_i(\cdot)\in\mathcal H.
$$

and for any function

$$
\varphi(\cdot)=\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i e_i(\cdot)\in\mathcal H
$$

we have $\forall y\in E$,

$$
\langle \varphi,K(\cdot,y)\rangle_{\mathcal H}
=\left\langle \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i e_i(\cdot),\sum_{i=1}^n \overline{e_i(y)},e_i(\cdot)\right\rangle_{\mathcal H}
=\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i e_i(y)
=\varphi(y).
$$

Hence any finite dimensional Hilbert space of functions has a reproducing kernel.

Example 2 Let $E=\mathbb N$ be the set of positive integers, and let $\mathcal H=\ell^2(E)$ be the set of complex sequences $(x_i)$ such that

$$
\sum_{i\in\mathbb N} |x_i|^2 <\infty.
$$

$\mathcal H$ is a Hilbert space with inner product, for $x=(x_i)$ and $y=(y_i)$.
$$
\langle x,y\rangle_{\ell^2(E)}=\sum_{i\in\mathbb N} x_i\overline{y_i}
$$

Let $K(i,j)=\delta_{ij}$ (the Kronecker symbol). Then,

$$
\forall j\in\mathbb N,\qquad K(\cdot,j)=(0,\ldots,0,1,0,\ldots)\in\mathcal H\quad \text{($1$ at the $j$-th place),}
$$

and

$$
\forall j\in\mathbb N\quad \forall x=(x_i),
\quad
\langle x,K(\cdot,j)\rangle _{\mathcal H}
=\sum _{i\in\mathbb N} x_i\delta _{ij}
=x_j.
$$

Hence $K$ is the reproducing kernel of $\mathcal H$.

Theorem 1. A Hilbert space of complex valued functions on $E$ has a reproducing kernel if and only if all the evaluation functions $e_t$, $t\in E$ are continuous on $\mathcal H$.

Proof : If $\mathcal H$ has a reproducing kernel $K$, then for any $t\in E$, we have for all $\varphi\in\mathcal H$

$$
e_t(\varphi)=\langle \varphi,K(\cdot,t)\rangle_{\mathcal H}.
$$

Thus, $e_t$ is linear and

$$
|e_t(\varphi)|
\le \Vert\varphi\Vert_{\mathcal H}\Vert K(\cdot,t)\Vert_{\mathcal H}
=\Vert\varphi\Vert_{\mathcal H},\langle K(\cdot,t),K(\cdot,t)\rangle_{\mathcal H}^{1/2}
=\Vert\varphi\Vert_{\mathcal H},[K(t,t)]^{1/2}.
$$

Hence $e_t$ is continuous.

Conversely, if $e_t$, $t\in E$ are continuous, by Riesz’s representation theorem, there exists a function $N_t(\cdot)\in\mathcal H$ such that for all $\varphi\in\mathcal H$

$$
\varphi(t)=e_t(\varphi)=\langle \varphi,N_t(\cdot)\rangle_{\mathcal H}
\qquad \text{for all } t\in E.
$$

Hence $K(s,t)=N_t(s)$ is the reproducing kernel of $\mathcal H$. $\square$

Remark: From the proof above, we get the above $e_t$ has norm $\Vert e_t\Vert_{\mathcal H’}=[K(t,t)]^{1/2}$.

Corollary 1 In a RKHS, a sequence converging in the norm sense converges pointwise to the same limit.

Proof : If $\varphi_n \xrightarrow{\Vert\cdot\Vert_{\mathcal H}} \varphi$, then for each $t\in E$,

$$
|\varphi_n(t)-\varphi(t)|
=|e_t(\varphi_n)-e_t(\varphi)|
\le \Vert e_t\Vert,\Vert \varphi_n-\varphi\Vert_{\mathcal H}
\to 0.
$$

Hence $\varphi_n\to\varphi$ pointwise. $\square$

Question: When is a complex-valued function $K$ defined on $E\times E$ a reproducing kernel?

Answer: If and only if $K$ is a positive type function.

Definition 2. (Positive type function). A function $K:E\times E\to\mathbb C$ is called a positive type function (or a positive definite function) if

$$
\forall n\ge 1,\qquad \forall (a_1,\ldots,a_n)\in\mathbb C^n,\qquad \forall (x_1,\ldots,x_n)\in E^n,
$$

we have

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{j=1}^n a_i\overline{a_j},K(x_i,x_j)\in \mathbb R^+,
$$

where $\mathbb R^+$ denotes the set of nonnegative real numbers.

It is worth noting that $K$ is a positive type function if and only if the matrix

$$
\bigl(K(x_i,x_j)\bigr)_{1\le i,j\le n}
$$

is positive definite for any choice of $n\in\mathbb N$ and $(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\in E^n$.

Examples.

(1) Any constant non negative function on $E\times E$ is of positive type.

Proof : If $K:E\times E\to\mathbb C$

$$
K(s,t)=c\ge 0
$$

then for all $n\in\mathbb N$, $(a_1,\ldots,a_n)\in\mathbb C^n$, $(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\in E^n$,

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{j=1}^n a_i\overline{a_j},K(x_i,x_j)
=c\sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{j=1}^n a_i\overline{a_j}
=c\left|\sum_{i=1}^n a_i\right|^2\in \mathbb R^+.
$$

(2). The delta function.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta:E\times E&\longrightarrow \mathbb C\\\
(x,y)&\longmapsto \delta_{xy}
=
\begin{cases}
1,&\text{if }x=y,\
0,&\text{if }x\ne y.
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

is of positive type.

Proof : Let $n\in\mathbb N$, $(a_1,\ldots,a_n)\in\mathbb C^n$, $(x_1,\ldots,x_n)\in E^n$.

Let $\{\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_p\}$ be the set of different values among $x_1,\ldots,x_n$.

We can write

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{j=1}^n a_i\overline{a_j},\delta_{x_i x_j}
=
\sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{x_j=x_i} a_i\overline{a_j}
=
\sum_{k=1}^p \sum_{x_i=x_j=\alpha_k} a_i\overline{a_j}
=
\sum_{k=1}^p \left|\sum_{x_j=\alpha_k} a_j\right|^2
\in \mathbb R^+.
$$

(3) The product $\alpha K$ of a positive type function $K$ with a non negative constant $\alpha$ is a positive type function.

Question: How to prove that a given function is of positive type?

Lemma 1. Let $\mathcal H$ be some Hilbert space with inner product $\langle \cdot,\cdot\rangle_{\mathcal H}$ and let $\varphi:E\to\mathcal H$. Then the function

$$
\begin{aligned}
K:E\times E&\longrightarrow \mathbb C\\\
(x,y)&\longmapsto K(x,y)=\langle \varphi(x),\varphi(y)\rangle_{\mathcal H}
\end{aligned}
$$

is of positive type.

Proof : The conclusion follows easily from the following equalities

$$
\sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{j=1}^n a_i\overline{a_j},K(x_i,x_j)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n\sum_{j=1}^n \langle a_i\varphi(x_i),a_j\varphi(x_j)\rangle_{\mathcal H}
=
\left\Vert \sum_{i=1}^n a_i\varphi(x_i)\right\Vert_{\mathcal H}^2
\in \mathbb R^+.\quad \square
$$

Lemma 1 tells us that writing

$$
K(x,y)=\langle \varphi(x),\varphi(y)\rangle_{\mathcal H}
$$

in some space $\mathcal H$ is sufficient to prove positive definiteness of $K$. $\square$

Reference

Berlinet, A., & Thomas-Agnan, C. (2011). Reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces in probability and statistics. Springer Science & Business Media.

The cover image of this article was taken at Lake Tekapo, New Zealand.
entry.summary: In this article we will introduce the definition of Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS).
entry.title: Definition of Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space

entry.content:
In this article, we will show some sufficient conditions for optimality and stability.

Theorem 1.12 Let $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ be compact and $c$ be $C^1$ cost function satisfying the twist condition on $\Omega imes \Omega$. Suppose that $\mu \in \mathcal{P}(\Omega)$ and $\varphi \in c ext{-cone}(\Omega)$ are given, that $\varphi$ is differentiable $\mu$-a.e. and that $\mu(\partial \Omega)=0$. Suppose that the map $T$ satisfies
$$
abla_x c(x,T(x))= abla \varphi(x).
$$
Then $T$ is optimal for the transport cost $c$ between the measures $\mu$ and
$$
u:=T_{\sharp}\mu.
$$

Proof : Since $\varphi \in c ext{-cone}(\Omega)$, $\varphi^{cc}=\varphi$, take $\psi=\varphi^c$ we have $\varphi=\psi^c$.

Fix $x_0\in \Omega$ such that $ abla \varphi(x_0)$ exists and $x_0 otin \partial \Omega$. By compactness and continuity $\exists\ y_0\in \Omega$ such that
$$
\varphi(x_0)=\inf_y [c(x_0,y)-\psi(y)]=c(x_0,y_0)-\psi(y_0).
$$
This gives,
$$
\varphi(x)\leq c(x,y_0)-\psi(y_0)
$$
and
$$
\varphi(x_0)=c(x_0,y_0)-\psi(y_0).
$$
and hence
$$
x\mapsto c(x,y_0)-\varphi(x)
$$
is minimal at $x=x_0$. As a consequence, we get
$$
abla \varphi(x_0)= abla_x c(x_0,y_0).
$$
By assumption, $y\mapsto abla_x c(x,y)$ is injective, and $ abla_x c(x_0,T(x_0))= abla \varphi(x_0)$. We have $y_0=T(x_0)$. This proves
$$
\varphi(x_0)+\psi(T(x_0))=c(x_0,T(x_0)),
$$
and this same equality is true for $\mu$-a.e. $x_0$.

Integrate with respect to $\mu$, we have
$$
\int_{\Omega} c(x,T(x))\ d\mu(x)
=
\int_{\Omega} \varphi(x)+\psi(T(x))\ d\mu(x)
=
\int_{\Omega} \varphi\ d\mu+\int_{\Omega} \psi\ d u.
$$
Hence
$$
\max(DP)\geq \int_{\Omega}\varphi\ d\mu+\int_{\Omega}\psi\ d u
=
\int_{\Omega} c(x,T(x))\ d\mu(x)
\geq \min(KP)\geq \max(DP).
$$
We have $T$ is optimal. $\square$

Theorem 1.13 Suppose $\mu\in \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R}^d)$ is such that
$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d} |x|^2\ d\mu(x)<+\infty,
$$
and that $u:\mathbb{R}^d o \mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}$ is convex and differentiable $\mu$-a.e.

Set
$$
T= abla u
$$
and suppose
$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d} |T(x)|^2\ d\mu(x)<+\infty.
$$
Then $T$ is optimal for the transport cost
$$
c(x,y):= rac{1}{2}|x-y|^2
$$
between the measures $\mu$ and
$$
u:=T_{\sharp}\mu.
$$

Proof : Note that for a convex function $u$
$$
u(x)+u^{\star}(y)\geq x\cdot y
$$
for all $x,y\in \mathbb{R}^d$ and
$$
u(x)+u^{\star}(y)=x\cdot y
$$
if $y= abla u(x)$.

Now consider $\gamma\in \Pi(\mu, u)$
$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d imes \mathbb{R}^d} x\cdot y\ d\gamma(x,y)
\leq
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d imes \mathbb{R}^d} (u(x)+u^{\star}(y))\ d\gamma(x,y)
=
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d} u(x)\ d\mu(x)+\int_{\mathbb{R}^d} u^{\star}(T(x))\ d\mu(x)=
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d} x\cdot T(x)\ d\mu(x)
=
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d imes \mathbb{R}^d} x\cdot y\ d\gamma_T.
$$

Therefore,
$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d imes \mathbb{R}^d} rac{1}{2}|x-y|^2\ d\gamma(x,y)
=
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d imes \mathbb{R}^d} rac{1}{2}(|x|^2+|y|^2)\ d\gamma(x,y)
-
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d imes \mathbb{R}^d} x\cdot y\ d\gamma(x,y)=
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d imes \mathbb{R}^d} rac{1}{2}(|x|^2+|y|^2)\ d\gamma_T
-
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d imes \mathbb{R}^d} x\cdot y\ d\gamma_T=
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d imes \mathbb{R}^d} rac{1}{2}|x-y|^2\ d\gamma_T,
$$

which shows $T$ is optimal. $\square$

Theorem 1.14 Suppose that $\gamma\in \mathcal{P}(X imes Y)$ is given, that $X$ and $Y$ are Polish spaces, that $c:X imes Y o \mathbb{R}$ is uniformly continuous and bounded, and that $\operatorname{supp}(\gamma)$ is $c$-CM.

Then $\gamma$ is an optimal transport plan between its marginals
$$
\mu=(\pi_x) _{\sharp}\gamma
\quad ext{and} \quad
u=(\pi_y) _{\sharp}\gamma
$$
for the cost $c$.

Proof : By Theorem 1.6, there exists a $c$-concave function $\varphi$ such that
$$
\operatorname{supp}(\gamma)\subset \{(x,y):\varphi(x)+\varphi^c(y)=c(x,y)\}
$$
and both $\varphi$ and $\varphi^c$ are continuous and bounded.

By Theorem 1.8, we know the duality result, hence
$$
\min(KP)\leq \int_{X imes Y} c(x,y)\ d\gamma
=
\int_{X imes Y} \varphi(x)+\varphi^c(y)\ d\gamma
=
\int_X \varphi\ d\mu+\int_Y \varphi^c\ d u\leq \max(DP)\leq \min(KP),
$$

which shows that $\gamma$ is optimal. $\square$

Definition 1.9 (Hausdorff distance) For a compact metric space $X$, we define the Hausdorff distance on pair of compact subsets of $X$ by
$$
d_H(A,B):=
\max\left\{
\max\{d(x,A):x\in B\},
\max\{d(x,B):x\in A\}
ight\}.
$$

Remark : We have the following equivalent definition.

  1. $d_H(A,B)=\max\{|d(x,A)-d(x,B)|:x\in X\}$.

  2. $d_H(A,B)=\inf\{\varepsilon>0:A\subset B_{\varepsilon},\ A_{\varepsilon}\supset B\}$, where $A_{\varepsilon},B_{\varepsilon}$ stands for the $\varepsilon$-neighborhood of $A$ and $B$ respectively.

Theorem 1.15 (Blaschke) $d_H$ is a distance.

Proposition 1.5 If $d_H(A_n,A) o 0$ and $\mu_n$ is a sequence of positive measures such that
$$
\operatorname{supp}(\mu_n)\subset A_n
$$
with $\mu_n ightharpoonup \mu$, then
$$
\operatorname{supp}(\mu)\subset A.
$$

Theorem 1.16 Suppose that $X$ and $Y$ are compact metric spaces and that $c:X imes Y o \mathbb{R}$ is continuous. Suppose that $\gamma_n\in \mathcal{P}(X imes Y)$ is a sequence of transport plan which are optimal for cost $c$ between their own marginals

$$
\mu_n=(\pi_x) _{\sharp}\gamma_n
\quad ext{and} \quad
u_n=(\pi_y) _{\sharp}\gamma_n
$$
and suppose $\gamma_n ightharpoonup \gamma$. Then

$$
\mu_n o \mu_{\infty}:=(\pi_x) _{\sharp} \gamma,
\quad
u_n o u:= (\pi_y) _{\sharp} \gamma,
$$

and $\gamma$ is optimal in the transport between $\mu$ and $ u$.

Proof : Set $\Gamma_n:=\operatorname{supp}(\gamma_n)$, up to subsequence, we can assume $\Gamma_n o \Gamma$ in Hausdorff distance. Each $\Gamma_n$ is a $c$-CM set, now we claim $\Gamma$ is also a $c$-CM set.

Fix $(x_1,y_1),\dots,(x_k,y_k)\in \Gamma$, there are points $(x_1^n,y_1^n),\dots,(x_k^n,y_k^n)\in \Gamma_n$ such that
$$
(x_i^n,y_i^n) o (x_i,y_i),\qquad i=1,2,\dots,k.
$$
The cyclical monotonicity of $\Gamma_n$ gives
$$
\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i^n,y_i^n)\leq \sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i^n,y_{\sigma(i)}^n).
$$
Take $n o\infty$
$$
\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_i)\leq \sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_{\sigma(i)}).
$$
Hence $\Gamma$ is a $c$-CM set. Since $\gamma_n ightharpoonup \gamma$ and $\Gamma_n o \Gamma$ in Hausdorff distance, we have
$$
\operatorname{supp}(\gamma)\subset \Gamma,
$$
which is also a $c$-CM set and implies the optimality of $\gamma$. $\square$

Notation : For a given cost $c:X imes Y o \mathbb{R}$ and $\mu\in \mathcal{P}(X)$, $ u\in \mathcal{P}(Y)$, define
$$
J_c(\mu, u):=
\min\left\{
\int_{X imes Y} c(x,y)\ d\gamma;\ \gamma\in \Pi(\mu, u)
ight\}.
$$

Theorem 1.17 Suppose that $X$ and $Y$ are compact metric spaces and that $c:X imes Y o \mathbb{R}$ is continuous. Suppose that $\mu_n\in \mathcal{P}(X)$, $ u_n\in \mathcal{P}(Y)$ with
$$
\mu_n o \mu
\qquad ext{and} \qquad
u_n o u.
$$
Then we have
$$
J_c(\mu_n, u_n) o J_c(\mu, u).
$$

Proof : Let $\gamma_n$ be an optimal transport plan from $\mu_n$ to $ u_n$ for the cost $c$. Up to subsequences, we can assume
$$
\gamma_n ightharpoonup \gamma.
$$
By Theorem 1.16, $\gamma$ is optimal for $\mu$ to $ u$. This means
$$
J_c(\mu_n, u_n)=\int_{X imes Y} c(x,y)\ d\gamma_n
o
\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\gamma
=
J_c(\mu, u),
$$
which proves the claim.

Theorem 1.18 Suppose that $X$ and $Y$ are compact metric spaces, and that $c:X imes Y o \mathbb{R}$ is continuous. Suppose that $\mu_n\in \mathcal{P}(X)$ and $ u_n\in \mathcal{P}(Y)$, with
$$
\mu_n o \mu
\qquad ext{and} \qquad
u_n o u.
$$
Let $(\varphi_n,\psi_n)$ be a pair of $c$-concave Kantorovich potentials for the cost $c$ in the transport from $\mu_n$ to $ u_n$. Then up to a subsequences, we have
$$
\varphi_n o \varphi
\qquad ext{and} \qquad
\psi_n o \psi
$$
where convergence is uniform and $(\varphi,\psi)$ is a pair of Kantorovich potentials for $\mu$ and $ u$.

Proof : It is easy to see that $\{\varphi_n\}$ and $\{\psi_n\}$ are equibounded and equicontinuity.

By Arzelà–Ascoli theorem, we have
$$
\varphi_n o \varphi,\qquad \psi_n o \psi
$$
up to a subsequence, and the convergence is uniform.

Since
$$
\varphi_n(x)+\psi_n(y)\leq c(x,y),
$$
we have
$$
\varphi(x)+\psi(y)\leq c(x,y).
$$
Moreover
$$
J_c(\mu_n, u_n)
=
\int_X \varphi_n\ d\mu_n+\int_Y \psi_n\ d u_n
o
\int_X \varphi\ d\mu+\int_Y \psi\ d u.
$$
By Theorem 1.17, we also have
$$
J_c(\mu_n, u_n) o J_c(\mu, u).
$$
Hence
$$
J_c(\mu, u)=\int_X \varphi\ d\mu+\int_Y \psi\ d u,
$$
which implies they are Kantorovich potentials. $\square$

Reference

Santambrogio, Filippo. Optimal transport for applied mathematicians. Birkäuser, NY 55.58-63 (2015): 94.

The cover image of this article was taken in Sydney, Australia.
entry.summary: In this article, we will show some sufficient conditions for optimality and stability.
entry.title: Sufficient Conditions for Optimality and Stability

entry.content:
In this article, we will prove the duality result for Kantorovich problem.

Theorem 1.8 Suppose that $X$ and $Y$ are Polish spaces, and that $c:X imes Y o \mathbb{R}$ is uniformly continuous and bounded. Then the problem $(\mathrm{DP})$ admits a solution $(\varphi,\varphi^c)$ and we have
$$
\max (\mathrm{DP}) = \min (\mathrm{KP}).
$$

Proof. Consider the minimization problem $(\mathrm{KP})$. Since $c$ is continuous, it admits a solution $\gamma$. By Theorem 1.7, the support $\Gamma=\operatorname{supp}(\gamma)$ is $c$-cyclically monotone. Since $c$ is real-valued, we can apply Theorem 1.6 and obtain the existence of a $c$-concave function $\varphi$ such that
$$
\Gamma \subset \{(x,y)\in X imes Y:\ \varphi(x)+\varphi^c(y)=c(x,y)\}.
$$

Since $c$ is continuous, we have $\varphi$ and $\varphi^c$ are continuous. Moreover, from the global upper bound on $\varphi^c$, which turns into a lower bound on $\varphi$,

$$
\varphi^c(y)=\inf_{x\in X}[c(x,y)-\varphi(x)].
$$

Symmetrically, we can also obtain upper bounds on $\varphi$ and lower bounds on $\varphi^c$, which proves that $\varphi$ and $\varphi^c$ are both continuous and bounded.

Hence, $(\varphi,\varphi^c)$ can be used as an admissible pair in $(\mathrm{DP})$. Consider now

$$
\int_X \varphi\ d\mu + \int_Y \varphi^c\ d u
= \int_{X imes Y} (\varphi(x)+\varphi^c(y))\ d\gamma
= \int_{\operatorname{supp}(\gamma)} (\varphi(x)+\varphi^c(y))\ d\gamma= \int_{\operatorname{supp}(\gamma)} c(x,y)\ d\gamma
= \int_{X imes Y} c(x,y)\ d\gamma.
$$

Hence

$$
(\mathrm{DP})\ge \int_X \varphi\ d\mu+\int_Y \varphi^c\ d u
= \int_{X imes Y} c(x,y)\ d\gamma
= (\mathrm{KP}).
$$

Since we already saw $(\mathrm{DP})\le (\mathrm{KP})$, we get $(\mathrm{DP})=(\mathrm{KP})$ and $(\varphi,\varphi^c)$ is optimal for $(\mathrm{DP})$. $\square$

Theorem 1.9 Let $\mu$ and $ u$ be probability measures over $\mathbb{R}^d$, and $c(x,y):= rac12|x-y|^2$. Suppose
$$
\int_X |x|^2\ \mu(dx)<+\infty
\qquad ext{and}\qquad
\int_Y |y|^2\ u(dy)<+\infty.
$$

Consider the following variant of $(\mathrm{DP})$:

$$
(\mathrm{DP-var})\qquad
\sup\left\{
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}\varphi\ d\mu+\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}\psi\ d u
:\ \varphi\in L^1(\mu),\ \psi\in L^1( u),\ \varphi\oplus\psi\le c
ight\}.
$$

Then $(\mathrm{DP-var})$ admits a solution $(\varphi,\psi)$, and the functions

$$
x\mapsto rac12|x|^2-\varphi(x)
\qquad ext{and}\qquad
y\mapsto rac12|y|^2-\psi(y)
$$

are convex and conjugate to each other for the Legendre transform. Moreover, we have

$$
\max (\mathrm{DP-var})=\min (\mathrm{KP}).
$$

Proof. Since $c$ is continuous, by Theorem 1.7 the support $\Gamma=\operatorname{supp}(\gamma)$ is $c$-cyclically monotone and then by Theorem 1.6, we get the existence of a pair $(\varphi,\psi)$ with $\psi=\varphi^c$ such that

$$
\varphi(x)+\psi(y)=c(x,y)
\qquad ext{for all }(x,y)\in \Gamma.
$$

From Proposition 1.2, we know

$$
x\mapsto rac12|x|^2-\varphi(x),\qquad
y\mapsto rac12|y|^2-\psi(y)
$$

are convex and conjugate to each other, hence bounded from below by a linear function; hence $\varphi$ and $\psi$ are bounded by a second order polynomial. Since

$$
\int_X |x|^2\ d\mu<+\infty
\qquad ext{and}\qquad
\int_Y |y|^2\ d u<+\infty,
$$

we know $\varphi_+\in L^1(\mu)$ and $\psi_+\in L^1( u)$.

Since

$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}\varphi\ d\mu+\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}\psi\ d u
=
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d imes\mathbb{R}^d} \varphi\oplus\psi\ d\gamma
=
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d imes\mathbb{R}^d} c(x,y)\ d\gamma
\ge 0,
$$

this proves

$$
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}\varphi\ d\mu,\ \int_{\mathbb{R}^d}\psi\ d u >-\infty.
$$

Hence $\varphi\in L^1(\mu)$, $\psi\in L^1( u)$.

Therefore

$$
\sup(\mathrm{DP-var})\ge
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}\varphi\ d\mu+\int_{\mathbb{R}^d}\psi\ d u
=
\int_{\mathbb{R}^d imes\mathbb{R}^d} c(x,y)\ d\gamma
=
\min(\mathrm{KP}).
$$

However, by $\varphi\oplus\psi\le c$, we know $\sup(\mathrm{DP-var})\le \min(\mathrm{KP})$. We get

$$
\max(\mathrm{DP-var})=\min(\mathrm{KP})
$$

and $(\varphi,\psi)$ is the solution of $(\mathrm{DP-var})$. $\square$

Lemma 1.8 Suppose that $c_k$ and $c$ are lower semi-continuous, and bounded from below and that $c_k$ converges increasingly to $c$. Then
$$
\lim_{k o\infty}\min\left\{\int_{X imes Y} c_k\ d\gamma:\ \gamma\in\Pi(\mu, u) ight\}
=
\min\left\{\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\gamma:\ \gamma\in\Pi(\mu, u) ight\}.
$$

Proof. Since $c_k\le c$, it is obvious that $LHS\le RHS$.

Now consider a sequence $\gamma_k\in\Pi(\mu, u)$ which is the minimizer for each cost $c_k$.

Up to subsequences, due to the tightness of $\Pi(\mu, u)$, we can suppose

$$
\gamma_k ightharpoonup \overline{\gamma}.
$$

Fix now an index $j$. Since for $k\ge j$ we have $c_k\ge c_j$, and then

$$
\lim_k \min\left\{\int_{X imes Y} c_k\ d\gamma:\ \gamma\in\Pi(\mu, u) ight\}
=
\lim_k \int_{X imes Y} c_k\ d\gamma_k
\ge
\liminf_k \int_{X imes Y} c_j\ d\gamma_k.
$$

Since $c_j$ is lower semi-continuous, and $\gamma_k ightharpoonup \overline{\gamma}$,

$$
\liminf_k \int_{X imes Y} c_j\ d\gamma_k
\ge
\int_{X imes Y} c_j\ d\overline{\gamma}.
$$

Hence, we obtain

$$
\lim_k \min\left\{\int_{X imes Y} c_k\ d\gamma:\ \gamma\in\Pi(\mu, u) ight\}
\ge
\int_{X imes Y} c_j\ d\overline{\gamma}.
$$

Since $j$ is arbitrary and

$$
\lim_{j o\infty}\int_{X imes Y} c_j\ d\overline{\gamma}
=
\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\overline{\gamma}
$$

by monotone convergence theorem, we also have

$$
\lim_k \min\left\{\int_{X imes Y} c_k\ d\gamma:\ \gamma\in\Pi(\mu, u) ight\}
\ge
\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\overline{\gamma}
\ge
\min\left\{\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\gamma:\ \gamma\in\Pi(\mu, u) ight\}.
$$

This completes the proof and $\overline{\gamma}$ is optimal for cost $c$. $\square$

Theorem 1.10 If $X,Y$ are Polish spaces and $c:X imes Y o \mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}$ is lower semi-continuous and bounded from below, then the duality formula
$$
\min(\mathrm{KP})=\sup(\mathrm{DP})
$$

holds.

Proof. Consider a sequence $c_k$ of bounded $k$-Lipschitz functions approaching $c$ increasingly. Then the dual result holds for $c_k$ and then

$$
\min\left\{\int_{X imes Y} c_k\ d\gamma:\ \gamma\in\Pi(\mu, u) ight\}
=
\max\left\{
\int_X \varphi\ d\mu+\int_Y \psi\ d u:\ \varphi\in C_b(X),\ \psi\in C_b(Y),\ \varphi\oplus\psi\le c_k
ight\}\le
\sup\left\{
\int_X \varphi\ d\mu+\int_Y \psi\ d u:\ \varphi\oplus\psi\le c
ight\}.
$$

Let $k o\infty$, by Lemma 1.8, we get the desired result. $\square$

Remark: For the cost $c$, we can not guarantee the existence of a maximizing pair $(\varphi,\psi)$.

Theorem 1.11 If $c$ is lower semi-continuous and $\gamma$ is an optimal transport plan, then $\gamma$ is concentrated on a $c$-CM set $\Gamma$.

Proof. By Theorem 1.10, we can take a sequence of maximizing pairs $(\varphi_n,\psi_n)$ in the dual problem, we have

$$
\int_{X imes Y} (\varphi_n(x)+\psi_n(y))\ d\gamma
=
\int_X \varphi_n(x)\ d\mu+\int_Y \psi_n(y)\ d u
o
\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\gamma,
$$

where $\gamma$ is optimal for $c$.

Moreover, since

$$
f_n(x,y):=c(x,y)-\varphi_n(x)-\psi_n(y)\ge 0,
$$

we have

$$
\int_{X imes Y} |f_n(x,y)|\ d\gamma
=
\int_{X imes Y} f_n(x,y)\ d\gamma
o 0
\qquad ext{as }n o\infty,
$$

that is $f_n o 0$ in $L^1(X imes Y,\gamma)$. Therefore, up to a subsequence, they also converge pointwisely $\gamma$-a.e. to $0$. Let $\Gamma\subset X imes Y$ be the set with $\gamma(\Gamma)=1$, where the convergence holds.

Take any $k$, $\sigma$ and $(x_1,y_1),\cdots,(x_k,y_k)\in \Gamma$, we have

$$
\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_i)
=
\lim_{n o\infty}\sum_{i=1}^k(\varphi_n(x_i)+\psi_n(y_i))
=
\lim_{n o\infty}\sum_{i=1}^k(\varphi_n(x_i)+\psi_n(y_{\sigma(i)}))
\le
\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_{\sigma(i)}),
$$

which proves that $\Gamma$ is $c$-CM. $\square$

Reference

Santambrogio, Filippo. Optimal transport for applied mathematicians. Birkäuser, NY 55.58-63 (2015): 94.

The cover image of this article was taken from the Luzern–Interlaken Express while passing Lake Lungern in Switzerland.
entry.summary: In this article, we will prove the duality result for Kantorovich problem.
entry.title: Duality Results for Kantorovich Problem

entry.content:
In this article, we will introduce some results for $c$-concavity and cyclical monotonicity, which will be used to prove the duality result in the next article.

Proposition 1.3 Let $c:X imes Y o \mathbb{R}$. For $\varphi:X o \mathbb{R} \cup \{-\infty\}$, we have
$$
\varphi^{c ar c} \ge \varphi.
$$

Moreover, the equality holds if and only if $\varphi$ is $c$-concave. In general, $\varphi^{c ar c}$ is the smallest $c$-concave function that is larger than $\varphi$.

Proof : First, we show $\varphi^{c ar c} \ge \varphi$. For any $x \in X$, $y \in Y$,
$$
\varphi^c(y)=\inf_{x\in X}[c(x,y)-\varphi(x)] \le c(x,y)-\varphi(x).
$$

Therefore,
$$
\varphi^{c ar c}(x)=\inf_{y\in Y}[c(x,y)-\varphi^c(y)]
\ge \inf_{y\in Y}[c(x,y)-(c(x,y)-\varphi(x))]=\varphi(x).
$$

Then, let us prove $\varphi^{c ar c}=\varphi$ if and only if $\varphi$ is $c$-concave. It is obvious that if $\varphi^{c ar c}=\varphi$ then $\varphi=(\varphi^c)^{ ar c}$, which shows that $\varphi$ is $c$-concave. If $\varphi$ is $c$-concave, then there exists a function $\zeta:Y o\mathbb{R}\cup\{-\infty\}$ such that
$$
\varphi=\zeta^{ ar c}.
$$

Hence
$$
\varphi^c=\zeta^{ ar c c}\ge \zeta,
$$
and then
$$
\varphi^{c ar c}\le \zeta^{ ar c}=\varphi,
$$
which implies $\varphi^{c ar c}=\varphi$.

Finally, if $\psi\ge \varphi$ and $\psi$ is $c$-concave, we need to show $\psi\ge \varphi^{c ar c}$. Suppose $\psi=\eta^{ ar c}$, then
$$
\psi=\eta^{ ar c}\ge \varphi
\Rightarrow \eta^{ ar c c}\le \varphi^c
\Rightarrow \eta\le \varphi^c
\Rightarrow \psi=\eta^{ ar c}\ge \varphi^{c ar c}.\quad \square
$$

Recall the sub-differential for convex functions

Definition 1.5 For every convex function $f:\mathbb{R}^d o\mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}$, we define its subdifferential at $x$ as the set
$$
\partial f(x):=\{p\in\mathbb{R}^d:\ f(y)\ge f(x)+p\cdot (y-x),\ orall y\in\mathbb{R}^d\}.
$$

Proposition 1.4 For every convex function $f:\mathbb{R}^d o\mathbb{R}$, we have

(1) $\partial f(x) eq \emptyset$ if $x\in \operatorname{int}(\operatorname{dom} f)$.

(2) If $f$ is differentiable at $x$, then $\partial f(x)=\{ abla f(x)\}$.

(3) $p\in \partial f(x)$ if and only if $x\in \partial f^\star(p)$ if and only if
$$
f(x)+f^\star(p)=x\cdot p.
$$

(4) If $p_1\in \partial f(x_1)$, $p_2\in \partial f(x_2)$, then
$$
(p_1-p_2)\cdot (x_1-x_2)\ge 0.
$$

Definition 1.6 Let us define the graph of the subdifferential of a convex function as
$$
\operatorname{Graph}(\partial f):=\{(x,p):\ p\in \partial f(x)\}
=\{(x,p):\ f(x)+f^\star(p)=x\cdot p\}.
$$

Definition 1.7 A set $A\subset \mathbb{R}^d imes \mathbb{R}^d$ is said to be cyclically monotone if for every $k\in \mathbb{N}$, every permutation $\sigma$ and every finite family of points $(x_1,p_1),\ldots,(x_k,p_k)\in A$, we have
$$
\sum_{i=1}^k x_i\cdot p_i \ge \sum_{i=1}^k x_i\cdot p_{\sigma(i)}.
$$

Note that if we take $k=2$, we get the usual definition of monotonicity
$$
x_1\cdot p_1+x_2\cdot p_2\ge x_1\cdot p_2+x_2\cdot p_1
\Leftrightarrow
(p_1-p_2)\cdot (x_1-x_2)\ge 0.
$$

By Rockafellar theorem, every cyclically monotone set is contained in the graph of the subdifferential of a convex function.

c-Cyclical Monotonicity

Definition 1.8 Once a function $c:X imes Y o \mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}$ is given, we say that a set $\Gamma\subset X imes Y$ is $c$-cyclically monotone (briefly $c$-CM) if for every $k\in\mathbb{N}$, every permutation $\sigma$ and every finite family of points $(x_1,y_1),\ldots,(x_k,y_k)\in \Gamma$, we have
$$
\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_i)\le \sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_{\sigma(i)}).
$$

Remark : Take $c(x,y)= rac12|x-y|^2$, we get $\Gamma$ is cyclically monotone.

Theorem 1.6 If $\Gamma eq \emptyset$ is a $c$-CM set in $X imes Y$ and $c:X imes Y o\mathbb{R}$ (note that $c$ is required not to take the value $+\infty$), then there exists a $c$-concave function $\varphi:X o \mathbb{R}\cup\{-\infty\}$ (different from the constant $-\infty$ function) such that
$$
\Gamma\subset \{(x,y)\in X imes Y:\ \varphi(x)+\varphi^c(y)=c(x,y)\}.
$$

Proof : Let us fix a point $(x_0,y_0)\in \Gamma$. For $x\in X$ set
$$
\varphi(x)=\inf \Big\{
c(x,y_n)-c(x_n,y_n)+c(x_n,y_{n-1})-c(x_{n-1},y_{n-1})
+\cdots +c(x_1,y_0)-c(x_0,y_0): n\in \mathbb{N},\ (x_i,y_i)\in \Gamma ext{ for all } i=1,2,\ldots,n
\Big\}.
$$

Since $c$ is real valued and $\Gamma$ is nonempty, $\varphi$ never takes the value $+\infty$.

If we set for $y\in Y$
$$
-\psi(y)=\inf \Big\{
-c(x_n,y)+c(x_n,y_n)-c(x_{n-1},y_n)+c(x_{n-1},y_{n-1})-\cdots +c(x_1,y_0)-c(x_0,y_0): n\in \mathbb{N},\ (x_i,y_i)\in \Gamma ext{ for all } i=1,2,\ldots,n,\ ext{and } y_n=y
\Big\}.
$$

By definition
$$
\psi(y)>-\infty
\Leftrightarrow
y\in (\pi_Y)(\Gamma)=\{y\in Y:\ \exists x\in X ext{ s.t. } (x,y)\in \Gamma\}.
$$

Next, we prove that $\varphi=\psi^c$, which implies $\varphi$ is $c$-concave. Indeed, if $y otin (\pi_Y)(\Gamma)$, $\psi(y)=-\infty$, then $c(x,y)-\psi(y)=+\infty$. Hence, we only need to consider $y\in (\pi_Y)(\Gamma)$. For $y\in (\pi_Y)(\Gamma)$,

$$
egin{aligned}
c(x,y)-\psi(y)
&=
c(x,y)+\inf \Big\{
-c(x_n,y)+c(x_n,y_n)-c(x_{n-1},y_n)+\cdots +c(x_1,y_0)-c(x_0,y_0):(x_i,y_i)\in \Gamma ext{ for all } i\ge 1,\ldots,n,\ ext{and } y_n=y
\Big\}\\
&=
\inf \Big\{
c(x,y_n)-c(x_n,y_n)+c(x_n,y_{n-1})-c(x_{n-1},y_{n-1})+\cdots +c(x_1,y_0)-c(x_0,y_0):(x_i,y_i)\in \Gamma ext{ for all } i=1,2,\ldots,n,\ ext{and } y_n=y
\Big\}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Take the infimum of $y=y_n$, we get
$$
\inf_y[c(x,y)-\psi(y)]=\varphi(x),
$$
that is
$$
\varphi=\psi^c.
$$

Then, we claim: $\varphi(x_0)\ge 0$ and hence $\varphi ot\equiv -\infty$. In fact, for all $n\in \mathbb{N}$ and $(x_i,y_i)\in \Gamma$, $i=1,2,\ldots,n$,
$$
c(x_0,y_n)-c(x_n,y_n)+c(x_n,y_{n-1})-c(x_{n-1},y_{n-1})+\cdots +c(x_1,y_0)-c(x_0,y_0)=
\sum_{i=0}^n c(x_{i+1},y_i)-\sum_{i=0}^n c(x_i,y_i)\ge 0.
$$

Hence $\varphi(x_0)\ge 0$.

Now, we need to show $\varphi(x)+\varphi^c(y)\ge c(x,y)$ for $(x,y)\in \Gamma$. Since $\varphi=\psi^c$ and we have $\varphi^c=\psi^{c ar c}\ge \psi$, we only need to show
$$
\varphi(x)+\psi(y)\ge c(x,y)
\qquad ext{for } (x,y)\in \Gamma.
$$

Fix $(x,y)\in \Gamma$. Since
$$
\varphi(x)=\psi^c(x)=\inf_{y\in (\pi_Y)(\Gamma)}[c(x,y)-\psi(y)],
$$
for all $\varepsilon>0$, there exists $ ar y\in (\pi_Y)(\Gamma)$ such that
$$
c(x, ar y)-\psi( ar y)<\varphi(x)+\varepsilon. ag{1}
$$

Claim :
$$
-\psi(y)\le -c(x,y)+c(x, ar y)-\psi( ar y).
$$

In fact, since $ ar y\in (\pi_Y)(\Gamma)$, there exists a chain
$$
(x_1,y_1),\ldots,(x_n,y_n)\in \Gamma,
\qquad ext{with } y_n= ar y.
$$

Since $(x,y)\in \Gamma$, we can add it to the chain above, and hence
$$
-\psi(y)\le -c(x,y)+c(x, ar y)-c(x_n, ar y)+c(x_n,y_n)-c(x_{n-1},y_n)+c(x_{n-1},y_{n-1})+\cdots +c(x_1,y_0)-c(x_0,y_0).
$$

Take the infimum, we get
$$
-\psi(y)\le -c(x,y)+c(x, ar y)-\psi( ar y). ag{2}
$$

Therefore, combined by (1) and (2), we have
$$
-\psi(y)<-c(x,y)+\varphi(x)+\varepsilon,
$$
by the arbitrarity of $\varepsilon>0$, we get
$$
\varphi(x)+\psi(y)\ge c(x,y).
$$

Since $\varphi^c\ge \psi$, which completes the prove. $\square$

Theorem 1.7 If $\gamma$ is an optimal transport plan for the cost $c$ and $c$ is continuous, then $\operatorname{Supp}(\gamma)$ is a $c$-CM set.

Proof : Suppose by contradiction that there exists $k$, $\sigma$ and $(x_1,y_1),\ldots,(x_k,y_k)\in \operatorname{Supp}(\gamma)$ such that
$$
\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_i)>\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_{\sigma(i)}).
$$

Take
$$
0<\varepsilon< rac{1}{2k}\left(\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_i)-\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_{\sigma(i)}) ight).
$$

By continuity of $c$, there exists $r>0$ such that for all $i=1,2,\ldots,k$, we have
$$
c(x,y)>c(x_i,y_i)-\varepsilon
\qquad ext{for all } (x,y)\in B(x_i,r) imes B(y_i,r),
$$
and
$$
c(x,y)<c(x_i,y_{\sigma(i)})+\varepsilon
\qquad ext{for all } (x,y)\in B(x_i,r) imes B(y_{\sigma(i)},r).
$$

Now, consider
$$
V_i:=B(x_i,r) imes B(y_i,r).
$$

Note that since $(x_i,y_i)\in \operatorname{supp}(\gamma)$, we have
$$
\gamma(V_i)>0
\qquad ext{for all } i=1,\ldots,k.
$$

Define measures

$$
\gamma_i:=\gamma \llcorner V_i/\gamma(V_i)
\quad ext{and} \quad
\mu_i=(\pi_x)_\sharp \gamma_i,\ u_i=(\pi_y)_\sharp \gamma_i.
$$

Take
$$
\varepsilon_0< rac{1}{k}\min_i \gamma(V_i).
$$

For every $i$, build a measure $\widetilde\gamma_i\in \Pi(\mu_i, u_{\sigma(i)})$ and define
$$
\widetilde\gamma:=\gamma-\varepsilon_0\sum_{i=1}^k \gamma_i+\varepsilon_0\sum_{i=1}^k \widetilde\gamma_i.
$$

Claim : $\widetilde\gamma\in \Pi(\mu, u)$ and
$$
\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\widetilde\gamma<\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\gamma.
$$

First, we check $\widetilde\gamma$ is a positive measure. It is sufficient to prove that $\gamma-\varepsilon_0\sum_{i=1}^k \gamma_i$ is positive, and for that, the condition
$$
\varepsilon_0\gamma_i< rac1k\gamma
$$
is enough.

Since
$$
\varepsilon_0\gamma_i= rac{\varepsilon_0}{\gamma(V_i)}\ \gamma\llcorner V_i,
$$
and
$$
rac{\varepsilon_0}{\gamma(V_i)}< rac1k,
$$
we get the desired result.

Now, let us check the marginals of $\widetilde\gamma$. We have

$$
(\pi_x)_\sharp \widetilde \gamma = \mu-\varepsilon_0 \sum _{i=1} ^k \mu_i+\varepsilon_0\sum _{i=1} ^k \mu_i = \mu
$$

and

$$
(\pi_y)_\sharp \widetilde\gamma = u-\varepsilon _0 \sum _{i=1} ^k u_i + \varepsilon_0 \sum _{i=1} ^k u _{\sigma(i)} = u.
$$

Finally, let us estimate
$$
\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\gamma-\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\widetilde\gamma.
$$
We have
$$
egin{aligned}
\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\gamma-\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\widetilde\gamma
&=
\varepsilon_0\sum_{i=1}^k \int_{X imes Y} c\ d\gamma_i
-\varepsilon_0\sum_{i=1}^k \int_{X imes Y} c\ d\widetilde\gamma_i\\
&\ge
\varepsilon_0\sum_{i=1}^k (c(x_i,y_i)-\varepsilon)
-\varepsilon_0\sum_{i=1}^k (c(x_i,y_{\sigma(i)})+\varepsilon)\\
&=
\varepsilon_0\left(
\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_i)-\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_{\sigma(i)})-2k\varepsilon
ight)>0,
\end{aligned}
$$

where we use the fact that $\gamma_i$ is concentrated on $B(x_i,r) imes B(y_i,r)$ and $\widetilde\gamma_i$ is concentrated on $B(x_i,r) imes B(y_{\sigma(i)},r)$. $\square$

Reference

Santambrogio, Filippo. Optimal transport for applied mathematicians. Birkäuser, NY 55.58-63 (2015): 94.

The cover image of this article was taken in Cromwell, New Zealand.
entry.summary: In this article, we will introduce some results for $c$-concavity and cyclical monotonicity, which will be used to prove the duality result in the next article.
entry.title: $c$-Concavity and Cyclical Monotonicity

entry.content:
In this article, we will consider $X=Y=\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ with $\Omega$ compact and $c(x,y)=h(x-y)$ for $h$ strictly convex.

From now on, we assume the duality result
$$
\min (\mathrm{KP})=\max (\mathrm{DP})
$$
holds true, which means for the optimal transport plan $\gamma$ and a Kantorovich potential $\varphi$, we have
$$
\int_{\Omega imes \Omega} c \ d\gamma
=
\int_{\Omega} \varphi \ d\mu + \int_{\Omega} \varphi^c \ d u.
$$

Since we have $\varphi(x)+\varphi^c(y)\le c(x,y)$ for every $x,y\in \Omega$, we have
$$
\varphi(x)+\varphi^c(y)=c(x,y)\quad \gamma-a.s.
$$

Furthermore, since $\varphi^c$ and $c$ are continuous, we have $\varphi(x)+\varphi^c(y)=c(x,y)$ on $\operatorname{supp}(\gamma)$.

Fix $(x_0,y_0)\in \operatorname{supp}(\gamma)$. By the definition of $\varphi^c$,
$$
\varphi^c(y_0)=\inf_{x\in \Omega}[c(x,y_0)-\varphi(x)]\le c(x,y_0)-\varphi(x)
\qquad orall x\in \Omega.
$$

On the other hand, since $(x_0,y_0)\in \operatorname{supp}(\gamma)$,
$$
\varphi(x_0)+\varphi^c(y_0)=c(x_0,y_0),
$$
which implies
$$
c(x_0,y_0)-\varphi(x_0)=\varphi^c(y_0)\le c(x,y_0)-\varphi(x)
\qquad orall x\in \Omega.
$$

Therefore
$$
x\longmapsto c(x,y_0)-\varphi(x)
$$
is minimal at $x=x_0$.

If $\varphi$ and $c(\cdot,y_0)$ are differentiable at $x_0$ and $x_0 otin \partial \Omega$, then the first order condition tells us
$$
abla_x c(x_0,y_0)- abla \varphi(x_0)=0,
$$
namely
$$
abla \varphi(x_0)= abla_x c(x_0,y_0).
$$

Proposition 1.1 If $c$ is $C^1$, $\varphi$ is a Kantorovich potential for the cost $c$ in the transport from $\mu$ to $ u$, and $(x_0,y_0)$ belongs to the support of an optimal transport plan $\gamma$. Then
$$
abla \varphi(x_0)= abla_x c(x_0,y_0),
$$
provided $\varphi$ is differentiable at $x_0$.

In particular, the gradients of two different Kantorovich potentials coincide on every point $x_0\in \operatorname{supp}(\mu)$ where both the potentials are differentiable.

Proof : The proof is contained in the above considerations.

Definition 1.4 (Twist condition) For $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^d$, we say that $c:\Omega imes \Omega o \mathbb{R}$ satisfies the twist condition whenever $c$ is differentiable w.r.t. $x$ at every point and the map
$$
y\longmapsto abla_x c(x,y)
$$
is injective for every $x_0\in \Omega$.

Remark By Proposition 1.1, we know for $(x_0,y_0)\in \operatorname{supp}(\gamma)$,
$$
abla_x c(x_0,y_0)= abla \varphi(x_0).
$$

If $c$ satisfies the twist condition, for given $x_0$, there is a unique $y_0$ such that
$$
(x_0,y_0)\in \operatorname{supp}(\gamma).
$$

This shows that $\gamma$ is concentrated on a graph.

For $c(x,y)=h(x-y)$ with $h$ strictly convex, if $\varphi$ and $h$ are differentiable at $x_0$ and $x_0-y_0$, respectively, and $x_0 otin \partial \Omega$. Then
$$
abla_x c(x,y)= abla h(x-y).
$$

If $ abla_x c(x,y_1)= abla_x c(x,y_2)$, then
$$
abla h(x-y_1)= abla h(x-y_2).
$$
Since $h$ is strictly convex,
$$
x-y_1=x-y_2,
$$
which implies $y_1=y_2$ and therefore $h$ satisfies twist condition.

Since
$$
abla \varphi(x_0)= abla_x c(x_0,y_0)= abla h(x_0-y_0),
$$
then
$$
x_0-y_0=( abla h)^{-1}( abla \varphi(x_0)).
$$

Hence
$$
y_0=x_0-( abla h)^{-1}( abla \varphi(x_0)).
$$

By Rademacher theorem, $\varphi$ is differentiable $\mathcal{L}^d$-a.e. If $\mu\ll \mathcal{L}^d$, then $ abla \varphi$ exists $\mu$-a.s.

Theorem 1.5 Given $\mu$ and $ u$ probability measures on a compact domain $\Omega\subset \mathbb{R}^d$, there exists an optimal transport plan $\gamma$ for the cost $c(x,y)=h(x-y)$ with $h$ strictly convex. It is unique and of the form $(id imes T)_\sharp \mu$ provided $\mu\ll \mathcal{L}^d$ and $\partial \Omega$ is negligible. Moreover, there exists a Kantorovich potential $\varphi$ and $T$ and the potentials $\varphi$ are linked by
$$
T(x)=x-( abla h)^{-1}( abla \varphi(x)).
$$

Proof : The proof is contained in the previous considerations and the uniqueness is by Proposition 1.1, which implies $ abla \varphi$ is unique, then the optimal $T$. $\square$

Remark. Every time we know that any optimal $\gamma$ must be induced by a map $T$, then we have uniqueness.

Proof : If we have two different plans
$$
\gamma_1=\gamma_{T_1}, \qquad \gamma_2=\gamma_{T_2}
$$
are optimal. Then
$$
ar{\gamma}:= rac12\gamma_1+ rac12\gamma_2
$$
is also optimal, but it cannot be induced by a map unless $T_1=T_2$, $\mu$-a.e., which gives a contradiction.

Indeed, let
$$
A:=\{x\in X:\ T_1(x) e T_2(x)\}.
$$
Suppose $\mu(A)>0$ and there exists a map $T:X o Y$ such that
$$
ar{\gamma}=\gamma_T=(id imes T)_\sharp\mu.
$$

For $B\in \mathcal{B}(X)$, consider
$$
\operatorname{Graph}(T|_B):=\{(x,T(x)):\ x\in B\}.
$$
We have
$$
egin{aligned}
ar{\gamma}(\operatorname{Graph}(T|_B)) &= (id imes T)_\sharp\mu ig(\{(x,T(x)):\ x\in B\} ig)\\
&=\mu ig((id imes T)^{-1}\{(x,T(x)):\ x\in B\} ig)\\
&=\mu(B).
\end{aligned}
$$

Define
$$
B_1=\{x\in A:\ T(x)=T_1(x)\}, \qquad B_2=\{x\in A:\ T(x)=T_2(x)\}.
$$
By the definition of $A$, we have $B_1\cap B_2=\varnothing$.

First, we claim $\mu ig(A\setminus (B_1\cup B_2) ig)=0.$

If not, $\mu(C)>0$, where $C=A\setminus (B_1\cup B_2)$. Consider
$$
E:=\operatorname{Graph}(T|_C)=\{(x,T(x)):\ x\in C\}.
$$
We have
$$
ar{\gamma}(E)=\mu(C)>0.
$$

However,
$$
\gamma_1(E)
=
\mu ig(\{x\in C:\ (x,T_1(x))\in E\} ig)
=
\mu ig(\{x\in C:\ T_1(x)=T(x)\} ig)=0.
$$
Similarly $\gamma_2(E)=0$. Therefore,
$$
ar{\gamma}(E)= rac12\gamma_1(E)+ rac12\gamma_2(E)=0,
$$
which gives a contradiction. Therefore
$$
\mu ig(A\setminus (B_1\cup B_2) ig)=0.
$$

Next, since
$$
A=B_1\cup B_2\cup A\setminus (B_1\cup B_2)
$$
and $B_1\cap B_2=\varnothing$,
$$
\mu(A)=\mu(B_1)+\mu(B_2)>0.
$$
Therefore, either $\mu(B_1)>0$ or $\mu(B_2)>0$. Without loss of generality, we assume $\mu(B_1)>0$.

Finally, consider
$$
F:=\operatorname{Graph}(T_2|_{B_1})=\{(x,T_2(x)):\ x\in B_1\}.
$$
We have
$$
\gamma_2(F)=\mu ig(\{x\in B_1:\ (x,T_2(x))\in F\} ig)=\mu(B_1),
$$
therefore
$$
ar{\gamma}(F)\ge rac12\gamma_2(F)= rac12\mu(B_1)>0.
$$

However,
$$
ar{\gamma}(F)
=
\mu ig(\{x\in X:\ (x,T(x))\in F\} ig)
=
\mu ig(\{x\in B_1:\ T(x)=T_2(x)\} ig)
=
\mu(\varnothing)=0,
$$
which gives a contradiction. $\square$

The quadratic case in $\mathbb{R}^d$: $c(x,y)= rac12|x-y|^2$

Proposition 1.2 Given a function $\chi:\mathbb{R}^d o \mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}$, let us define
$$
u_\chi:\mathbb{R}^d o \mathbb{R}\cup\{+\infty\}
$$
$$
u_\chi(x)= rac12|x|^2-\chi^c(x).
$$

Then we have
$$
u_{\chi^c}=(u_\chi)^\star
$$
where $f^\star$ denotes the Legendre-Fenchel transform of $f$.

In particular, a function $\varphi$ is $c$-concave if and only if
$$
x\longmapsto rac12|x|^2-\varphi(x)
$$
is convex and lower semi-continuous.

Proof :
$$
egin{aligned}
u_{\chi^c}(x)& = rac12|x|^2-\chi^c(x)\\
&=\sup_y\left[ rac12|x|^2- rac12|x-y|^2+\chi(y) ight]\\
&=
\sup_y\left[x\cdot y-\left( rac12|y|^2-\chi(y) ight) ight]\\
&=(u_\chi)^\star.
\end{aligned}
$$

If $\varphi$ is $c$-concave, which means there exists a function $\chi$ s.t.
$$
\varphi=\chi^c,
$$
then
$$
u_\varphi(x)= rac12|x|^2-\varphi(x)=(u_\chi)^\star
$$
which is convex and lower semi-continuous.

Conversely, if $u_\varphi= rac12|x|^2-\varphi(x)$ is convex and lower-semi-continuous, then there exists a function $\chi$ such that
$$
u_\varphi=\chi^\star.
$$

Then
$$
egin{aligned}
\varphi(x)&= rac12|x|^2-\chi^\star=
rac12|x|^2-\sup_y[x\cdot y-\chi(y)]\\
&=
\inf_y\left[ rac12|x-y|^2-\left( rac12|y|^2-\chi(y) ight) ight]=
\left( rac12|y|^2-\chi(y) ight)^c,
\end{aligned}
$$

which shows that $\varphi$ is $c$-concave. $\square$

By Theorem 1.5, there exists a optimal transport map
$$
T(x)=x- abla \varphi(x)= abla\left( rac12|x|^2-\varphi(x) ight)= abla u_\varphi(x).
$$

By Proposition 1.2, $u_\varphi$ is convex.

Reference

Santambrogio, Filippo. Optimal transport for applied mathematicians. Birkäuser, NY 55.58-63 (2015): 94.

The cover image of this article was taken upon arriving in Kagoshima, Japan, aboard a Royal Caribbean cruise.
entry.summary: In this article, we will consider $X=Y=\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ with $\Omega$ compact and $c(x,y)=h(x-y)$ for $h$ strictly convex.
entry.title: The case $c(x,y)=h(x-y)$ for $h$ Strictly Convex and the Existence of an Optimal Transport Map $T$

entry.content:
In this article, we will talk about the dual problem Kantorovich.

Let us express the constraint $\gamma \in \Pi(\mu, u)$ in the following way: if $\gamma \in M_+(X imes Y)$ then we have
$$
\sup_{\varphi,\psi}
\left[
\int_X \varphi \ d\mu
+\int_Y \psi \ d u
-\int_{X imes Y} (\varphi(x)+\psi(y)) \ d\gamma
ight]
=
egin{cases}
0, & ext{if } \gamma \in \Pi(\mu, u),\\
+\infty, & ext{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
$$
where $\varphi,\psi$ are bounded continuous.

Proof : If $\gamma \in \Pi(\mu, u)$, it is trivial. If $\gamma otin \Pi(\mu, u)$, then there exists a pair $(\varphi_0,\psi_0)$ such that
$$
I(\varphi_0,\psi_0)
:=
\int_X \varphi_0 \ d\mu
+\int_Y \psi_0 \ d u
-\int_{X imes Y} (\varphi_0(x)+\psi_0(y)) \ d\gamma
eq 0.
$$
W.L.O.G. we can assume $I>0$, then if we choose $(a\varphi_0,a\psi_0)$ for $a>0$,
$$
I(a\varphi_0,a\psi_0)=a I(\varphi_0,\psi_0).
$$
Hence
$$
\mathrm{LHS}=\sup_{\varphi,\psi} I(\varphi,\psi)=+\infty.
$$

Hence the Kantorovich problem is equivalent to the following problem
$$
\min_{\gamma \in M_+(X imes Y)}
\left\{
\int_{X imes Y} c \ d\gamma
+\sup_{\varphi,\psi}
\left[
\int_X \varphi \ d\mu
+\int_Y \psi \ d u
-\int_{X imes Y} (\varphi(x)+\psi(y)) \ d\gamma
ight]
ight\}.
$$

Consider interchanging $\sup$ and $\inf$
$$
\sup_{\varphi,\psi}
\left\{
\int_X \varphi \ d\mu
+\int_Y \psi \ d u
+\inf_{\gamma \in M_+(X imes Y)}
\left[
\int_{X imes Y} igl(c(x,y)-(\varphi(x)+\psi(y)) igr)\ d\gamma
ight]
ight\}.
$$

Now we denote $(\varphi \oplus \psi)(x,y):=\varphi(x)+\psi(y)$, then the infimum in $\gamma$ can be written as
$$
\inf_{\gamma \in M_+(X imes Y)} \int_{X imes Y} (c-\varphi\oplus\psi)\ d\gamma
=
egin{cases}
0, & ext{if } \varphi\oplus\psi \le c ext{ on } X imes Y,\\
-\infty, & ext{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
$$

Proof : Let
$$
\widetilde{J}(\gamma):=\int_{X imes Y} (c-\varphi\oplus\psi)\ d\gamma.
$$
If $\varphi\oplus\psi \le c$ on $X imes Y$, then for every $\gamma \in M_+(X imes Y)$,
$$
\widetilde{J}(\gamma)\ge 0.
$$
Since for all $a>0$,
$$
\widetilde{J}(a\gamma)=a\widetilde{J}(\gamma),
$$
we have
$$
\inf_{\gamma \in M_+(X imes Y)} \widetilde{J}(\gamma)=0.
$$

If $\varphi+\psi \le c$ is not true, then for every $\gamma \in M_+(X imes Y)$, $\widetilde{J}(\gamma)<0$. Since for all $a>0$ we have $\widetilde{J}(a\gamma)=a\widetilde{J}(\gamma)$, we have
$$
\inf_{\gamma \in M_+(X imes Y)} \widetilde{J}(\gamma)=-\infty.
$$

Therefore, we get the following dual optimization problem:

Problem 1.3. Given $\mu \in \mathcal{P}(X)$, $ u \in \mathcal{P}(Y)$ and the cost function $c:X imes Y o [0,+\infty]$, we consider the problem
$$
(\mathrm{DP})\qquad
\sup
\left\{
\int_X \varphi \ d\mu + \int_Y \psi \ d u
\ \middle|\
\varphi \in C_b(X),\ \psi \in C_b(Y),\ \varphi\oplus\psi \le c
ight\}.
$$

Lemma 1.3. $\sup(\mathrm{DP}) \le \min(\mathrm{KP})$.

Proof : For $\gamma \in \Pi(\mu, u)$, then for every $\varphi \in C_b(X)$, $\psi \in C_b(Y)$ with $\varphi\oplus\psi \le c$, we have
$$
\int_X \varphi \ d\mu + \int_Y \psi \ d u
=
\int_{X imes Y} \varphi\oplus\psi \ d\gamma
\le
\int_{X imes Y} c \ d\gamma.
$$

Definition 1.1. Given a function $\chi:X o \overline{\mathbb R}$, we define its $c$-transform (also called $c$-conjugate function)
$$
\chi^c:Y o \overline{\mathbb R}
$$
by
$$
\chi^c(y):=\inf_{x\in X}\ [c(x,y)-\chi(x)].
$$

We also define the $\overline{c}$-transform of $\xi:Y o \overline{\mathbb R}$ by
$$
\xi^{\overline{c}}:X o \overline{\mathbb R}
$$
$$
\xi^{\overline{c}}(x):=\inf_{y\in Y}\ [c(x,y)-\xi(y)].
$$

Moreover, we say that a function $\psi$ defined on $Y$ is $\overline{c}$-concave if there exists $\chi:X o \overline{\mathbb R}$ such that $\psi=\chi^c$, and a function $\varphi$ on $X$ is said to be $c$-concave if there exists $\xi:Y o \overline{\mathbb R}$ such that $\varphi=\xi^{\overline{c}}$.

We denote by $c ext{-conc}(X)$ and $\overline{c} ext{-conc}(Y)$ the sets of $c$-concave and $\overline{c}$-concave functions, respectively.

(When $X=Y$, and $c$ is symmetric, the distinction between $c$ and $\overline{c}$ will play no more any rule and will be dropped as soon as possible.)

Definition 1.2. A function $f:X o \mathbb R$ is said to have modulus of continuity $\omega:\mathbb R_+ o \mathbb R_+$ if for all $x,y\in X$

$$
|f(x)-f(y)|\le \omega(d(x,y)).
$$

Lemma 1.4. If $c:X imes Y o \overline{\mathbb R}$ is continuous and finite on a compact set, then there exists an increasing continuous function $\omega:\mathbb R_+ o \mathbb R_+$ with $\omega(0)=0$ such that
$$
|c(x,y)-c(x’,y’)|\le \omega igl(d(x,x’)+d(y,y’) igr).
$$

Proof : Let $\omega:\mathbb R_+ o \mathbb R_+$ be defined by
$$
\omega(t):=
\sup\left\{
|c(x,y)-c(x’,y’)|
\ :\
d(x,x’)+d(y,y’)\le t
ight\}.
$$
It is easy to see that $\omega(0)=0$ and $\omega(t)$ is increasing.

Let $r=d(x,x’)+d(y,y’)$, then
$$
|c(x,y)-c(x’,y’)|\le \omega(r)=\omega igl(d(x,x’)+d(y,y’) igr).
$$
Since $c$ is uniformly continuous on its domain, $\omega$ is continuous.

Lemma 1.5. Let $(f_\alpha)$ be a family of functions, all satisfying the same modulus of continuity $\omega:\mathbb R_+ o \mathbb R_+$,

$$
|f_\alpha(x)-f_\alpha(x’)|\le \omega(d(x,x’)),\qquad orall \alpha.
$$

Then
$$
f:=\inf_\alpha f_\alpha
$$
also satisfies the same modulus of continuity.

Proof : For each $\alpha$,
$$
f_\alpha(x)\le f_\alpha(x’)+\omega(d(x,x’)),
$$
which implies
$$
f(x)\le f_\alpha(x’)+\omega(d(x,x’))
$$
since $f\le f_\alpha$. Taking the infimum at the RHS, we get
$$
f(x)\le f(x’)+\omega(d(x,x’)).
$$
Interchanging $x$ and $x’$, we obtain
$$
|f(x)-f(x’)|\le \omega(d(x,x’)).
$$

Lemma 1.6. If $c:X imes Y o \overline{\mathbb R}$ has the modulus of continuity $\omega:\mathbb R_+ o \mathbb R_+$, then $\chi^c$ shares the same modulus of continuity.

Proof : Since
$$
\chi^c(y)=\inf_{x\in X} igl(c(x,y)-\chi(x) igr)=: \inf_{x\in X} g_x(y),
$$
and for $x\in X$, $y,y’\in Y$,
$$
|g_x(y)-g_x(y’)|=|c(x,y)-c(x,y’)|\le \omega(d(y,y’)),
$$
by Lemma 1.5 we get the desired result.

Lemma 1.7. Let
$$
DP(\varphi,\psi):=\int_X \varphi \ d\mu+\int_Y \psi \ d u.
$$
For $\varphi\in C_b(X)$, $\psi\in C_b(Y)$ with $\varphi\oplus\psi\le c$. Then
$$
DP(\varphi,\varphi^c)\ge DP(\varphi,\psi).
$$

Proof : Since $\varphi\oplus\psi\le c$, we have
$$
\psi(y)\le c(x,y)-\varphi(x).
$$
Furthermore
$$
\psi(y)\le \inf_x [c(x,y)-\varphi(x)]=\varphi^c(y).
$$
Hence
$$
DP(\varphi,\psi)\le \int_X \varphi \ d\mu+\int_Y \varphi^c \ d u = DP(\varphi,\varphi^c).
$$

Remark. Similarly, we will have
$$
DP(\varphi,\psi)\le DP(\varphi,\varphi^c)\le DP(\varphi^{c\overline{c}},\varphi^c)\le DP(\psi^{\overline{c}},\psi^{\overline{c}c})\le DP(\varphi^{c\overline{c}c},\varphi^{c\overline{c}})\le \cdots
$$

Theorem 1.4. Suppose that $X$ and $Y$ are compact and $c$ is continuous. Then there exists a solution $(\varphi,\psi)$ to problem $(\mathrm{DP})$ and it has the form $\varphi\in c ext{-conc}(X)$, $\psi\in \overline{c} ext{-conc}(X)$ and $\psi=\varphi^c$. In particular,
$$
\max(\mathrm{DP})
=
\max_{\varphi\in c ext{-conc}(X)}
\left[
\int_X \varphi \ d\mu+\int_Y \varphi^c \ d u
ight].
ag{$\star$}
$$

Proof : From the considerations above, we can take a maximizing sequence $(\varphi_n,\psi_n)$ and improve it by means of $c$- and $\overline{c}$-transforms, and we can assume they have the same modulus of continuity as $c$. Instead of renaming the sequence, we will still call $(\varphi_n,\psi_n)$ the new sequence obtained after these transforms.

By Ascoli-Arzelà’s theorem, we only need to check the equiboundedness. For every constant $a\in \mathbb R$,
$$
DP(\varphi,\psi)=DP(\varphi+a,\psi-a)
$$
and
$$
(\varphi+a)\oplus(\psi-a)\le c.
$$
Since $\varphi_n$ is continuous on a compact set and then bounded, W.L.O.G. we can assume
$$
\min \varphi_n=0
$$
and then we get
$$
\max \varphi_n\le \omega(\operatorname{diam} X).
$$
If we have chosen $\psi_n=\varphi_n^c$, we also have
$$
\psi_n(y)=\inf_{x\in X}[c(x,y)-\varphi_n(x)]
\in
igl[\min c-\omega(\operatorname{diam} X),\ \max c igr].
$$

Hence equibounded. By Ascoli-Arzelà’s theorem, there exists a subsequence
$$
\varphi_{n_k} o \varphi,\qquad \psi_{n_k} o \psi\quad ext{(uniform convergence).}
$$

Therefore
$$
\int_X \varphi_{n_k}\ d\mu+\int_Y \psi_{n_k}\ d u
o
\int_X \varphi\ d\mu+\int_X \psi\ d u.
$$
and
$$
\varphi_{n_k}(x)+\psi_{n_k}(y)\le c(x,y)
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\varphi(x)+\psi(y)\le c(x,y).
$$
This shows that $(\varphi,\psi)$ is an admissible pair for $(\mathrm{DP})$ and then optimal.

And if $\psi eq \varphi^c$, we can improve the objective functional in $(\mathrm{DP})$, which contradicts to $(\varphi,\psi)$ is optimal. Similarly $\varphi=\psi^{\overline{c}}$.

Hence $\psi=\varphi^c\in \overline{c} ext{-conc}(X)$ and similarly $\varphi=\psi^{\overline{c}}\in c ext{-conc}(X)$.

Remark. If $\min(\mathrm{KP})=\max(\mathrm{DP})$, we also have
$$
\min(\mathrm{KP})
=
\max_{\varphi\in c ext{-conc}(X)}
\left[
\int_X \varphi \ d\mu+\int_Y \varphi^c \ d u
ight]
$$
which also shows that the minimum value of $(\mathrm{KP})$ is a convex function of $(\mu, u)$ as it is a supremum of linear functionals.

Definition 1.3. The functions $\varphi$ realizing the maximum in $(\star)$ are called Kantorovich potentials for the transport from $\mu$ and $ u$.

Reference

Santambrogio, Filippo. Optimal transport for applied mathematicians. Birkäuser, NY 55.58-63 (2015): 94.

The cover image of this article was taken in Auckland, New Zealand.
entry.summary: In this article, we will talk about the dual problem Kantorovich.
entry.title: Dual Problem of Kantorovich

entry.content:
In this article, we will talk about the problems of Monge and Kantorovich and the corresponding existence of solutions.

Monge Problem

Problem 1.1 (Monge Problem). Given $\mu \in \mathcal{P}(X)$, $ u \in \mathcal{P}(Y)$ and a Borel measurable cost function
$$
c:X imes Y o [0,+\infty],
$$
solve
$$
(\mathrm{MP}) \qquad \inf \left\{ M(T):=\int_X c(x,T(x))\ d\mu(x) \ \middle|\ T_\sharp\mu= u ight\},
$$
where
$$
T_\sharp\mu(A)=\mu igl(T^{-1}(A) igr)
$$
for all $A \in \mathcal{B}(Y)$. Find also the optimal transport map $T$.

Remark. Difficulty of Monge Problem is that the constraint
$$
\{T:X o Y \mid T_\sharp\mu= u\}
$$
is often not closed under weak convergence.

Example. $X=[0,2\pi]$, $\mu=\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\ dx$, $T_n(x)=\sin(nx)$, $Y=\mathbb R$.

Claim. For all $n \in \mathbb{N}$,

$$
(T_n)_\sharp\mu= u,
$$

where

$$
u(dy)= rac{1}{\pi\sqrt{1-y^2}}\ 1_{(-1,1)}(y)\ dy.
$$

Proof : For all $\varphi \in C_b(\mathbb R)$,

$$
egin{aligned}
\int_Y \varphi(y)\ ((T_n)_\sharp\mu)(dy)
&=
\int_X \varphi(\sin nx)\ \mu(dx)
=
rac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}\varphi(\sin nx)\ dx \\
&= rac{1}{2\pi n} \int_0^{2\pi n}\varphi(\sin u)\ du
= rac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}\varphi(\sin u)\ du
=\int_Y \varphi(y)\ u(dy).
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $(T_n)_\sharp\mu= u$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$.

However, by Riemann-Lebesgue Lemma, in $L^p[0,2\pi]$, $1 \leq p < +\infty$, we have for all $g \in L^q[0,2\pi]$
$$
\int_0^{2\pi} g(x)\sin(nx)\ dx o 0.
$$
Hence
$$
T_n ightharpoonup 0.
$$
But for $T=0$, $T_\sharp\mu=\delta_0 eq u$, which shows that
$$
\left\{ T \in L^p([0,2\pi]) \ ; \ T_\sharp\mu= u ight\}
$$
is not closed under weak topology of $L^p[0,2\pi]$.

Kantorovich Problem

Problem 1.2 (Kantorovich Problem). Given $\mu \in \mathcal{P}(X)$, $ u \in \mathcal{P}(Y)$, and a Borel measurable cost function
$$
c:X imes Y o [0,\infty],
$$
find
$$
(\mathrm{KP}) \qquad \inf \left\{ K(\gamma):=\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\gamma \ \middle|\ \gamma \in \Pi(\mu, u) ight\},
$$
where $\Pi(\mu, u)$ is the set of so-called transport plans, i.e.
$$
\Pi(\mu, u)
=
\left\{
\gamma \in \mathcal{P}(X imes Y)
\ \middle|\
(\pi_x)_\sharp\gamma=\mu,\ (\pi_y)_\sharp\gamma= u
ight\},
$$
where $\pi_x$ and $\pi_y$ are two projections of $X imes Y$ onto $X$ and $Y$, respectively.

Remark. If $T_\sharp\mu= u$ then the plan
$$
\pi=(\mathrm{id} imes T)_\sharp\mu \in \Pi(\mu, u).
$$
Conversely, if $\pi=(\mathrm{id} imes T)_\sharp\mu \in \Pi(\mu, u)$, then $T_\sharp\mu= u$.

Proof : First, if $T_\sharp\mu= u$, then for all $A \in \mathcal{B}(X)$, $B \in \mathcal{B}(Y)$,
$$
\pi(A imes Y)
=
((\mathrm{id} imes T)_\sharp\mu)(A imes Y)
=
\mu igl((\mathrm{id} imes T)^{-1}(A imes Y) igr)
=
\mu(A),
$$
and
$$
\pi(X imes B)
=
((\mathrm{id} imes T)_\sharp\mu)(X imes B)
=
\mu igl((\mathrm{id} imes T)^{-1}(X imes B) igr)
=
\mu(T^{-1}(B))
=
u(B).
$$
Hence $\pi \in \Pi(\mu, u)$.

Second, if $\pi \in \Pi(\mu, u)$, then for all $B \in \mathcal{B}(Y)$,
$$
u(B)
=
\pi(X imes B)
=
((\mathrm{id} imes T)_\sharp\mu)(X imes B)
=
\mu igl((\mathrm{id} imes T)^{-1}(X imes B) igr)
=
\mu(T^{-1}(B)).
$$
Hence $T_\sharp\mu= u$. $\square$

Remark. In the problem of Monge, it may happen that there is no map $T$ such that $T_\sharp\mu= u$. Indeed, if $\mu=\delta_{x_0}$, $x_0 \in X$ and $ u$ is any measure that is different from an atomic measure on one atom, then there is no such map, as
$$
T_\sharp\mu=\delta_{T(x_0)}.
$$
But the set $\Pi(\mu, u)$ is always non-empty, as
$$
\mu \otimes u \in \Pi(\mu, u).
$$

Weak-$\star$ Convergence and Weak Convergence

Let $\mathcal{M}(X)$ be the set of finite signed measures on $X$. By Riesz Representation theorem, if $X$ is separable and locally compact metric space, let $\mathcal{X}=C_0(X)$ be the set of continuous functions on $X$ vanishing at infinity, i.e. $f \in C_0(X)$ if and only if $f \in C_b(X)$ and for every $\varepsilon>0$, there exists a compact subset $K \subset X$ such that
$$
|f(x)|<\varepsilon
$$
on $X \setminus K$.

And endow this space with sup norm. Since $C_0(X) \subset C_b(X)$, then $C_0(X)$ is a closed subset of $C_b(X)$ also a Banach space. By Riesz Representation Theorem for all $\xi \in \mathcal{X}’$, there exists a unique $\lambda \in \mathcal{M}(X)$ such that
$$
\langle \xi,\varphi angle=\int_X \varphi\ d\lambda
$$
for every $\varphi \in \mathcal{X}=C_0(X)$.

Moreover $\mathcal{X}^\prime \simeq \mathcal{M}(X)$ endowed with the norm $\Vert\lambda\Vert:=|\lambda|(X).$

For signed measures $\mathcal{M}(X)$:

  • weak-$\star$ convergence: the convergence in the duality with $C_0(X)$, that is $\mu_n \overset{\mathrm{weak}-\star}{\longrightarrow} \mu$ if and only if for all $\varphi \in C_0(X)$

$$
\int_X \varphi\ d\mu_n o \int_X \varphi\ d\mu
\qquad ext{as } n o \infty.
$$

  • weak convergence (also called narrow convergence): $\mu_n ightharpoonup \mu$ if and only if for all $\varphi \in C_b(X)$

$$
\int_X \varphi\ d\mu_n o \int_X \varphi\ d\mu
\qquad ext{as } n o \infty.
$$

If $X$ is compact, then $C_0(X)=C_b(X)=C(X)$, weak-$\star$ convergence and weak convergence are the same.

Existence of Solutions

Theorem 1.1. Suppose that $X,Y$ are compact and $c:X imes Y o \mathbb R$ is continuous. Then the Kantorovich Problem admits a minimizer.

Proof : We just need to show that the set $\Pi(\mu, u)$ is compact and that
$$
\gamma \longmapsto K(\gamma)=\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\gamma
$$
is continuous. Since $X imes Y$ compact, $C_0(X imes Y)=C_b(X imes Y)=C(X imes Y)$, $\gamma \mapsto K(\gamma)$ is continuous.

For the compactness, take $(\gamma_n) \subset \Pi(\mu, u)$, since they are probability measures, they are bounded in $\mathcal{M}(X imes Y) \simeq (C(X imes Y))’$. By Banach-Alaoglu theorem, there exists a subsequence $\gamma_{n_k}$,
$$
\gamma_{n_k} \overset{\mathrm{weak}-\star}{\longrightarrow} \gamma \in \mathcal{M}(X imes Y).
$$
Since $X imes Y$ is compact,
$$
\gamma(X imes Y)
=
\int_{X imes Y} 1\ d\gamma
=
\lim_{k o \infty}\int_{X imes Y} 1\ d\gamma_{n_k}
=
1.
$$
Hence $\gamma \in \mathcal{P}(X imes Y)$. Moreover, for all $\varphi \in C(X)$, and since $\gamma_{n_k} \in \Pi(\mu, u)$,
$$
\int_{X imes Y} \varphi\ d\gamma_{n_k}
=
\int_X \varphi\ d\mu.
$$
Let $k o \infty$, we have
$$
\int_{X imes Y} \varphi\ d\gamma
=
\int_X \varphi\ d\mu.
$$
Hence $(\pi_x)_\sharp\gamma=\mu$ and similarly, $(\pi_y)_\sharp\gamma= u$. Hence $\gamma \in \Pi(\mu, u)$. Therefore, $\Pi(\mu, u)$ is compact. $\square$

Lemma 1.1. Let $f:X o \mathbb R \cup \{+\infty\}$ be a function bounded from below. Then $f$ is lower semi-continuous if and only if there exists a sequence $f_k$ of $k$-Lipschitz functions such that for every $x \in X$, $f_k(x)$ converges increasingly to $f(x)$. Furthermore, $f_k$ can also be made bounded.

Proof : On the one hand, if
$$
f(x)=\lim_{k o \infty} f_k(x)
$$
for all $x \in X$ with $f_k$ $k$-Lipschitz and increasing, then
$$
f=\sup_k f_k
$$
is also lower semi-continuous.

On the other hand, if $f$ is lower semi-continuous and bounded from below, define
$$
f_k(x)=\inf_y \{ f(y)+k\ d(x,y) \}.
$$
It is easy to show that $f_k$ is $k$-Lipschitz. For fixed $x \in X$, $f_k(x)$ is increasing and we have
$$
\inf f \leq f_k(x) \leq f(x).
$$
We just need to show that
$$
\ell:=\lim_{k o \infty} f_k(x)=\sup_k f_k(x)=f(x).
$$
Suppose by contradiction $\ell<f(x)$, which implies $\ell<+\infty$. For every $k$, choose $y_k$ such that
$$
f(y_k)\leq f(y_k)+k\ d(x,y_k)<f_k(x)+ rac{1}{k}\leq \ell+ rac{1}{k}. ag{1}
$$
We get
$$
d(y_k,x)\leq rac{\ell+ rac{1}{k}-f(y_k)}{k}\leq rac{C}{k}
$$
(since $\ell<\infty$, $f$ bounded from below). Hence $y_k o x$. Let $k o \infty$ in $(1)$. Since $f$ is lower semi-continuous,
$$
f(x)\leq \liminf_{k o \infty} f(y_k)\leq \lim_{k o \infty} f_k(x)=\ell,
$$
which gives a contradiction.

Finally, $f_k$ can be made bounded by taking $f_k \wedge k$. $\square$

Lemma 1.2. If $f:X o \mathbb R \cup \{+\infty\}$ is a lower semi-continuous function, bounded from below on a metric space $X$, then the functional
$$
J:\mathcal{M}_+(X) o \mathbb R\cup\{+\infty\}
$$
defined on positive measures on $X$ through
$$
J(\mu):=\int_X f\ d\mu
$$
is lower semi-continuous for the weak convergence of measures.

Proof : By Lemma 1.1, there exists a sequence $f_k$ of continuous and bounded functions converging increasingly to $f$. Then write
$$
J(\mu)=\sup_k J_k(\mu):=\int_X f_k\ d\mu
$$
(Actually $J_k \leq J$ and $J_k(\mu) o J(\mu)$ for every $\mu$ by monotone convergence.)

Since every $J_k$ is continuous for the weak convergence, hence $J$ is a lower semi-continuous functional. $\square$

Theorem 1.2. Let $X$ and $Y$ be compact metric spaces, $\mu \in \mathcal{P}(X)$, $ u \in \mathcal{P}(Y)$ and
$$
c:X imes Y o \mathbb R\cup\{+\infty\}
$$
be lower semi-continuous and bounded from below. Then the Kantorovich Problem $(\mathrm{KP})$ admits a solution.

Proof : By Lemma 1.2
$$
\gamma \mapsto K(\gamma)=\int_{X imes Y} c\ d\gamma
$$
is lower semi-continuous. By the proof of Theorem 1.1, we know $\Pi(\mu, u)$ is compact. $\square$

Theorem 1.3. Let $X$ and $Y$ be Polish spaces, $\mu \in \mathcal{P}(X)$, $ u \in \mathcal{P}(Y)$ and
$$
c:X imes Y o \mathbb R\cup\{+\infty\}
$$
is lower semi-continuous. Then $(\mathrm{KP})$ admits a solution.

Proof : We only need to prove the compactness of $\Pi(\mu, u)$.

Since $\mu$ and $ u$ are finite Borel measures on Polish spaces, by Ulam theorem, $\mu$ and $ u$ are tight, there exist $K_X \subset X$ and $K_Y \subset Y$ such that
$$
\mu(X \setminus K_X)< rac{\varepsilon}{2},
\qquad
u(Y \setminus K_Y)< rac{\varepsilon}{2}.
$$
Then the set $K_X imes K_Y$ is compact in $X imes Y$ and for any $(\gamma_n)\subset \Pi(\mu, u)$
$$
\gamma_n igl((X imes Y)\setminus (K_X imes K_Y) igr)
\leq
\gamma_n igl((X \setminus K_X) imes Y igr)+\gamma_n igl(X imes (Y \setminus K_Y) igr)=
\mu(X \setminus K_X)+ u(Y \setminus K_Y)
<
